236:
ZUKUNFT ADVISORY
Governance / Capital Markets / Digital Asset Strategy

May 18, 2026

Vanessa A. Countryman, Secretary, U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, 100 F Street, NE, Washington, DC 20549-1090

Re: Semiannual Reporting; File No. S7-2026-15; Release Nos. 33-11414; 34-105368; 39-2563; IC-36140

Dear Ms. Countryman:

I write in response to the Commission's request for comment on the proposed amendments to Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13 that would permit domestic reporting companies to file a semiannual report on new Form 10-S in lieu of two of three quarterly reports on Form 10-Q. I have practiced as a corporate and securities lawyer for more than twenty-seven years, including as a partner at Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer, Paul Weiss Rifkind Wharton & Garrison, K&L Gates, and Smith Anderson, and in senior in-house roles as chief legal officer building legal functions for public market readiness. The perspective I bring to this comment letter is shaped by having advised, and served as de facto general counsel to, public companies across the full range of the reporting company population. That has included large accelerated filers with deep institutional ownership, established analyst coverage, active capital markets programs, and the full institutional disclosure architecture that mature public company status requires. It has also included smaller reporting companies, emerging growth companies, and recently public issuers operating in earlier stages of that institutional progression, where the quarterly reporting cadence often feels disproportionate to the operational tempo of the business. The disclosure liability gap and on-ramp questions I address in this letter come from running quarterly reporting cycles at both ends of that range, not from one end. I submit this letter in my capacity as Founder of Zukunft Advisory.

I do not oppose the proposal. The Commission's policy rationale for offering reporting companies an alternative to the existing quarterly cadence is defensible, and the cost-reduction objective is real for the subset of issuers for whom quarterly reporting has always been disproportionate to the operational tempo of the business. My concern with the proposal as currently drafted is narrower and, I believe, addressable. The proposing release does not adequately engage with what replaces the quarterly cadence for companies that elect semiannual reporting and continue to communicate with the market on a quarterly basis, which the release itself contemplates and which I believe will be the majority of electing issuers in practice. The institutional scaffolding that has historically made quarterly market communications defensible is anchored in the existence of the Form 10-Q. Removing the formal filing without addressing what supports the continuing communications creates a disclosure liability gap that the proposing release does not name and that the comment file to date has not addressed in operational terms.

This letter offers five contributions. First, it identifies the disclosure liability gap created by the hybrid approach the proposing release contemplates. Second, it surfaces the audit committee, disclosure committee, and auditor review questions that follow from removing the formal quarterly filing while continuing quarterly market communications. Third, it proposes a four-component framework that semiannual filers should be expected to implement on top of the election to preserve the institutional discipline that has historically protected quarterly communications. Fourth, it addresses who the election actually fits and raises the on-ramp question that follows from the bifurcation the proposal will create between large accelerated filers (who will overwhelmingly not elect) and smaller, newer, and development-stage issuers (who will). Fifth, it responds directly to several of the Commission's numbered requests for comment and offers three specific recommendations for what the final release should contain.

**I. The disclosure liability gap created by the hybrid approach.** The proposing release explicitly contemplates that semiannual filers may continue to provide voluntary quarterly information, including through earnings releases, conference calls, and voluntary inclusion of quarterly financial information within the Form 10-S filing itself. The release describes this as a hybrid approach that may represent a middle ground for companies seeking to balance cost savings with market expectations. What the release does not engage with is that the hybrid approach, in practice, will be the operating posture of the substantial majority of electing issuers, not a middle ground reserved for those with specific institutional ownership profiles or capital markets needs.

The reasons are structural. Analyst coverage of U.S. public companies is built around quarterly models. Institutional investors maintain valuation frameworks that require quarterly revenue, earnings, and guidance updates. Lenders incorporate financial covenant certification and reporting requirements into credit agreements that often track the Form 10-Q schedule by default. Boards have organized their meeting calendars and audit committee cadences around the quarterly filing cycle for decades. Investor relations functions have built their annual programs around four earnings cycles. The semiannual election does not change any of those expectations on day one.

Most issuers that elect semiannual reporting will therefore continue to communicate with the market on a quarterly cadence. They will issue earnings releases. They will hold earnings calls. They will provide guidance and update it. What changes is not the existence of those communications. What changes is the institutional scaffolding that historically sat behind them.

The Form 10-Q is a Section 18 filed document. It carries CEO and CFO certifications under Sections 302 and 906 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002. It is reviewed by the independent auditor under PCAOB AS 4105. It runs through a disclosure committee process required by Item 307 and Item 308 of Regulation S-K. It is approved by the audit committee. Item 4 of Form 10-Q requires an express attestation regarding the effectiveness of disclosure controls and procedures. The Form 10-Q is the artifact produced at the end of that process, but the process itself is the institutional discipline that makes the quarterly communications surrounding the filing defensible.

When a company communicates quarterly without producing a Form 10-Q, that scaffolding is not automatically present. The earnings release is furnished under Item 2.02 of Form 8-K, not filed under Section 18. The auditor has performed no interim review because there is no periodic report to review. The disclosure committee may or may not have run a full process. The CEO and CFO sign off on the release, but they make no certifications under Sections 302 or 906 because no underlying periodic report exists to certify. The forward-looking statements in the prepared remarks rest on a cautionary statement framework that, in the absence of a contemporaneous Form 10-Q, has no reaffirmed risk factor universe to anchor to. This gap is not theoretical. It is exactly the fact pattern that the plaintiffs' securities class action bar will construct cases around. The defense of a Rule 10b-5 case predicated on quarterly forward-looking statements, guidance, or operational commentary has historically relied substantially on the architecture of the periodic reporting process. Plaintiffs' counsel will identify the gap. The Commission should anticipate it.

**II. The audit committee, disclosure committee, and auditor review questions.** Three operational questions follow directly from the disclosure liability gap.

*A. The auditor review question.* The auditing profession has not yet articulated publicly how it will approach interim review of quarterly financial information for semiannual filers that continue to issue quarterly earnings releases. Under AU-C 930 / AS 4105, an interim review is generally requested in connection with a periodic report containing reviewed interim financial information. Whether and on what terms a registered public accounting firm will perform interim review work outside the context of a Form 10-Q filing, and at what fee level, is unresolved. If the auditor will not perform that review without a Form 10-Q, the issuer will be communicating quarterly without the auditor scaffolding. If the auditor will perform the review, the cost savings the proposing release contemplates are at least partly illusory. The Commission should engage with the PCAOB and the AICPA in advance of adoption to clarify the auditor's posture.

*B. The audit committee oversight question.* Audit committees have organized their oversight cadence around the quarterly Form 10-Q cycle for decades. Moving to two formal cycles per fiscal year does not reduce the audit committee's oversight responsibility. It removes the rhythm that has historically structured how that responsibility is discharged. An audit committee that elects to maintain quarterly review discipline despite the move to semiannual filings will be doing more work, not less, because it will be running its full process without the external statutory deadline. The Commission should consider express guidance to audit committees of electing companies, and whether stock exchange listing standards require corresponding amendment.

*C. The disclosure committee question.* Item 307 of Regulation S-K requires an express attestation by the principal executive and principal financial officers regarding the effectiveness of disclosure controls and procedures, made in each Form 10-Q and Form 10-K. For semiannual filers, the Item 307 attestation will be made only in the Form 10-S and Form 10-K. The off-cycle quarter earnings release and conference call will not be supported by a contemporaneous Item 307 attestation. The Commission should consider whether semiannual filers should be required to disclose, in their Form 10-S or Form 10-K, whether the disclosure committee has reviewed any voluntary quarterly information released during the off-cycle period to a standard substantially equivalent to the standard applied to a Form 10-Q.

**III. A proposed framework for semiannual filers that continue quarterly market communications.** Four components, which I respectfully suggest the Commission consider either as elements of the final rule, as elements of companion staff guidance, or as expectations articulated in the adopting release's discussion of the hybrid approach.

*Component 1: A voluntary quarterly business update document, treated as a deliberate disclosure artifact.* Semiannual filers that issue quarterly earnings releases should be expected to treat those releases as deliberate disclosure artifacts subject to substantially the same internal review and approval process applied to a Form 10-Q.

*Component 2: Auditor involvement calibrated to the issuer's risk profile.* In the absence of profession-wide clarification, electing issuers should be expected to obtain interim review work in connection with quarterly financial information released to the market wherever the issuer's circumstances make such review prudent.

*Component 3: A preserved disclosure committee and audit committee process operating on a quarterly cadence.* Audit committee charters, disclosure committee charters, and internal disclosure controls policies of electing issuers should be updated to reflect the semiannual reporting cadence and to make clear that off-cycle quarters receive substantially the same governance treatment as on-cycle quarters.

*Component 4: A redesigned investor relations cadence.* Removing two formal quarterly earnings cycles from the issuer's annual disclosure calendar does not reduce the underlying demand for institutional engagement. It diffuses that engagement across a larger number of less structured touchpoints, each of which carries its own Regulation FD exposure. Spreading the same engagement demand across a larger surface area, without the concentrated discipline of four well-rehearsed earnings cycles per year, increases Regulation FD risk.

**IV. Who the election actually fits, and the on-ramp question.** The proposing release identifies smaller reporting companies, emerging growth companies, and companies in industries where investors focus on business and regulatory developments rather than interim financial results as the natural beneficiaries of the election. I agree as far as it goes. The question the proposing release does not engage with, and the question I respectfully submit the Commission should address in the final release, is what happens to these issuers when they outgrow the conditions that made the election attractive in the first place. The natural arc of a public company that goes public as a smaller reporting company or an emerging growth company is one in which, over time, the company grows revenues, attracts analyst coverage, develops capital markets ambitions, takes on bank financing, accumulates institutional ownership, and eventually graduates to accelerated or large accelerated filer status. The disclosure cadence the company runs on during that progression is part of the institutional infrastructure that supports it.

The honest answer is that the on-ramp question is bidirectional. The election will help some smaller reporting companies and emerging growth companies that would otherwise be unable to bear the cost of being public at all. It will also box in some smaller reporting companies and emerging growth companies that elect in early and then find themselves underprepared for the institutional demands of mature public company status when they reach it. The Commission should be candid about that tradeoff in the final release.

Two structural consequences follow. First, larger issuers will overwhelmingly not elect semiannual reporting. Second, the resulting bifurcation between quarterly filers (predominantly mature, larger-cap, institutionally-owned) and semiannual filers (predominantly smaller, newer, development-stage) is a structural change to the public company population that the proposing release does not address as such.

**V. Responses to specific requests for comment.** I respond below to several of the Commission's numbered requests for comment.

*On voluntary quarterly disclosures and the hybrid approach.* The substantial majority of electing issuers will continue to provide quarterly information in some form because the underlying market demand for that information will not change. The Commission should provide guidance, ideally in the adopting release, on the standard of care expected in connection with voluntary quarterly disclosures.

*On internal controls and quarterly review.* Removing two of those points per fiscal year does not change the underlying control environment, but it removes two of the formal occasions on which the control environment is evaluated and documented. For accountant reviews, the profession's posture is unresolved, and the Commission should engage with the PCAOB and the AICPA to clarify it in advance of adoption.

*On insider trading policies and trading window practices.* Semiannual reporting will significantly complicate trading window administration for electing issuers and will require careful redesign of Rule 10b5-1 plan practices. Most public company insider trading policies define blackout periods by reference to the quarterly earnings cycle and the Form 10-Q filing. For semiannual filers, the absence of two of those quarterly anchors will require either an extension of blackout periods (effectively reducing trading windows substantially) or a redesign of the blackout framework around the voluntary quarterly disclosure cadence.

*On capital markets activity and registered offerings.* The Regulation S-X staleness amendments included in the proposal address the technical question but do not address the practical market question, which is whether underwriters, investors, and rating agencies will be willing to participate in offerings supported only by semiannual financial information. The 135-day rule under existing PCAOB standards for negative assurance in comfort letters is the most concrete example of this dependency. The Commission should consider whether final adoption should be coordinated with PCAOB action to ensure that semiannual filers have a workable path to access the capital markets between Form 10-S filings.

**VI. Specific recommendations for the final release.**

First, the adopting release should articulate the expectation that semiannual filers that continue to provide voluntary quarterly information apply substantially the same disclosure committee and audit committee discipline to that information as they would apply to a Form 10-Q.

Second, the Commission should coordinate with the PCAOB and the AICPA to clarify the auditing profession's posture on interim review work for semiannual filers in advance of final adoption.

Third, the Commission should require electing issuers to disclose, in their Form 10-S and Form 10-K, the issuer's practice with respect to off-cycle quarterly market communications, including whether the issuer issues quarterly earnings releases, whether disclosure committee and audit committee review processes are applied to those communications, and whether interim review work is performed by the issuer's independent registered public accounting firm in connection with those communications.

**VII. Conclusion.** The Commission's objective in proposing optional semiannual reporting is defensible. My concern is not with the election. It is with the operational consequences of the hybrid approach the proposing release contemplates, with the disclosure liability gap that follows from removing the formal quarterly filing without addressing what supports the continuing quarterly market communications that will, in practice, accompany the election for most electing issuers, and with the on-ramp question that follows from the bifurcation the proposal will produce within the public company population.

Respectfully submitted,

ZUKUNFT ADVISORY, LLC
Margaret Rosenfeld, Founder
mrosenfeld@zukunftadvisory.com

253:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Anthony Langenstein

Date: May 19, 2026

Vanessa A. Countryman, Secretary
Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street, NE
Washington, DC 20549-1090

Subject: File Number S7-2026-15 — Technical Assessment and Opposition to Proposed Semiannual Reporting Framework

Dear Secretary Countryman,

I am writing to formally log my opposition to the Commission's rule proposal to permit public issuers the option to transition from quarterly financial reporting to a semiannual reporting framework on Form 10-S. While acknowledging the Commission's intent to mitigate short-termism and lower corporate compliance costs, empirical evidence and economic principles suggest this framework will degrade market efficiency, elevate cost of capital, and exacerbate information asymmetry.

The introduction of a semiannual reporting cycle creates systemic risks across several critical vectors:

- Erosion of the U.S. Market Gold Standard and Foreign Investment: The U.S. capital markets are globally recognized as the gold standard precisely because of our rigorous, transparent, and frequent disclosure regimes. Investors worldwide allocate capital to U.S. equities because they trust the integrity and timeliness of the data. Lowering our standards to match the less frequent reporting cycles of international markets will homogenize our ecosystem, strip away our competitive advantage, and ultimately diminish the attractiveness of U.S. markets to vital foreign direct investment.

- Compounding Operational and Supply Chain Risks: In a highly interconnected global economy, operational disruptions — such as severe supply chain bottlenecks, inflationary pressures, or sudden macroeconomic shifts — develop rapidly. A six-month reporting gap ensures that these material risks will compound entirely hidden from public view. Instead of the market making orderly, continuous price discoveries based on quarterly updates, investors will be exposed to severe, delayed material shocks that disrupt portfolio risk management.

- Information Asymmetry and Bid-Ask Spreads: In the fields of financial economics and market microstructure, reducing reporting frequency directly increases information asymmetry. Institutional market participants possess the capital to acquire proprietary, alternative data streams to bridge a six-month information vacuum, whereas individual retail investors do not. Consequently, market makers will likely widen bid-ask spreads to protect against adverse selection, increasing transaction costs and reducing liquidity for the broader market.

- Delayed Price Discovery and Volatility Clustering: Quarterly reporting facilitates continuous, incremental price discovery. Extending the reporting interval to six months will cause material structural data to compound behind a curtain of corporate confidentiality. Consequently, when earnings are finally disclosed, the price adjustment process will be discrete rather than continuous. This will result in heightened idiosyncratic volatility and severe price gaps upon announcement, destabilizing secondary market trading.

- Insufficiency of Form 8-K as an Interim Substitute: The proposal relies on voluntary, interim financial disclosures furnished via Form 8-K to maintain market communication. This mechanism is structurally flawed. Disclosures under Item 2.02 or 7.01 lack the standardized presentation, mandatory GAAP reconciliations, and strict disclosure controls and procedures (DCP) inherent to Form 10-Q. Relying on fragmented, unstandardized updates diminishes comparability across peers and increases the cognitive processing burden on market analysts.

- Agency Costs and Corporate Governance: Quarterly reporting acts as a vital commitment mechanism that aligns manager and shareholder incentives. Extending the reporting window increases the duration of unmonitored management discretion. This lag delays the market's ability to identify capital misallocation, operational drift, or deteriorating internal controls, thereby elevating agency costs and eroding governance discipline.

The proposed reduction in regulatory costs is heavily outweighed by the negative externalities imposed on capital allocation efficiency, market transparency, and global competitiveness. I strongly encourage the Commission to preserve the current mandated quarterly disclosure regime under Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13.

Thank you for analyzing these technical considerations.

Sincerely,
Anthony Langenstein
Individual Investor

257:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Lance Graven

Subject: File Number S7-2026-15 - Opposition to Proposed Rule Change for Semiannual Reporting
Dear Secretary Countryman,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Securities and Exchange Commission's proposal to permit public companies to transition from quarterly financial reporting to semiannual reporting. While I understand the stated goal is to reduce corporate compliance costs and alleviate short-term market pressures, I believe this change will severely harm individual investors, degrade market transparency, and increase financial volatility.
Quarterly reporting is a cornerstone of the modern, transparent U.S. capital markets. Moving to a six-month reporting cycle introduces several significant risks:
Increased Information Asymmetry: Individual retail investors rely heavily on timely, standardized financial disclosures to make informed investment decisions. Extending the reporting window to six months creates a massive information vacuum. This gap will disproportionately benefit institutional investors who have the resources to utilize alternative data streams, leaving retail investors at a severe disadvantage.
Heightened Market Volatility: Waiting six months to receive audited financial updates means that unexpected operational shifts, supply chain disruptions, or macroeconomic impacts will build up behind closed doors. When these results are finally released, the market reactions are likely to be far more volatile and disruptive than the gradual adjustments seen under a quarterly system.
Reduced Management Accountability: Quarterly oversight forces corporate executives to maintain strict financial discipline and operational accountability throughout the fiscal year. A semiannual framework reduces this regular check-and-balance, potentially allowing corporate inefficiencies or strategic missteps to go unnoticed by the public for too long.
Ineffectiveness of Voluntary Updates: Allowing companies to provide informal interim updates via Form 8-K is an inadequate substitute for standardized Form 10-Q filings. Fragmented, non-standardized disclosures make it difficult for investors to accurately compare performance across companies or sectors.
For these reasons, I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain the current quarterly reporting framework. Protecting the integrity, transparency, and fairness of our markets for all participants must remain the SEC's highest priority.
Thank you for your time and consideration of my comments.
Sincerely,
 Lance Graven

25:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Isaiah Owolabi

RE: Proposed Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies
File No. S7-2026-15
Submitted by: Isaiah Owolabi, LL.M. (FinTech Law & Regulation)
Founder and CEO, ESGine | Originator, ESG-as-Code, Washington, DC

I want to start by saying I think this proposal is a step in the right direction. Quarterly reporting has created a rhythm that works well for some companies and feels like unnecessary overhead for others. Giving companies the flexibility to choose makes sense.

But I want to flag something that I do not think has been addressed clearly enough in the proposal, and it matters a great deal for how companies interpret this change.

Reporting frequency and disclosure obligations are two completely different things. One is about when you file. The other is about what the law requires you to say when you do.

A company that switches from quarterly to semiannual reporting does not reduce what it has to disclose. It simply consolidates those disclosures into fewer filing events. The legal obligations remain exactly the same. The clause does not move. Only the calendar does.

I raise this because I have spent the past several years working at the intersection of law, technology, and ESG compliance. Through ESGine and ESG-as-Code, I have built a platform that evaluates ESG and climate disclosures against specific regulatory clauses across seven major frameworks including SEC Climate, CSRD, CARB SB253, CARB SB261, ISSB, UK FCA, and SFDR. The companies using it are not struggling with how often to report. They are struggling with knowing exactly what each regulatory clause requires and whether their disclosure actually satisfies it.

That is the harder problem. And it does not get easier with semiannual reporting. If anything it gets more concentrated because everything that would have been spread across three quarterly reports now has to land correctly in one semiannual report.

My concern is that some companies will read this proposal as a signal that the regulatory burden is easing. It is not. The burden is being reorganized. That is a meaningful distinction and I think the final rule should say so explicitly.

I would respectfully ask the Commission to include clear language in any final rule stating that the election of semiannual reporting does not reduce, defer, or modify any substantive disclosure obligation under the federal securities laws. Companies need to understand that frequency is a business decision. Compliance is a legal one.

I would also encourage the Commission to use this moment to highlight the importance of systematic, auditable compliance infrastructure. A company that can trace every disclosure to the specific regulatory clause that requires it is a company that can file quarterly, semiannually, or annually with confidence. That traceability is what regulators actually need to see. It is also what ESG-as-Code was built to provide.

I am happy to discuss any of this further if it would be helpful to the Commission staff.

Respectfully submitted,
Isaiah Owolabi, LL.M. (FinTech Law & Regulation)
Founder and CEO, ESGine
Originator, ESG-as-Code
Washington, DC

26:
I respectfully oppose the proposal to reduce the frequency of required reporting for public issuers from quarterly to semi-annual. While I appreciate the objective of easing compliance burdens, I believe the proposal would have several unintended consequences that could ultimately undermine the transparency, efficiency, and integrity of U.S. capital markets, which are currently the world standard.

First, reducing the cadence of financial reporting would materially diminish the timeliness of information available to investors. Quarterly reporting plays a critical role in providing up-to-date insight into an issuer's financial performance, liquidity, and emerging risks. Moving to a semi-annual model would lengthen the information gap between disclosures, increasing uncertainty and potentially impairing investors' ability to make informed decisions. In volatile or rapidly evolving industries this loss of timely visibility could be particularly significant.

Second, the proposal may lead to increased market volatility and information asymmetry. In the absence of regular, structured reporting, market participants may rely more heavily on informal disclosures, selective communications, or non-GAAP metrics, which may not be subject to the same level of rigor or internal control. This dynamic could disproportionately benefit well-connected or institutional investors, to the detriment of broader market fairness.

Third, quarterly reporting serves as a key discipline mechanism for issuers, reinforcing the importance of robust internal controls, close processes, and ongoing financial oversight. Reducing reporting frequency could inadvertently weaken these governance practices, leading to less frequent monitoring of financial trends, delayed identification of issues, and increased risk of error or misstatement accumulating over longer reporting periods.

Additionally, from an audit and assurance perspective, interim reporting supports continuous engagement between auditors and management, facilitating earlier identification and resolution of complex accounting issues. A shift to semi-annual reporting could compress review timelines and increase the complexity and risk associated with interim reviews and annual audits.

Finally, the U.S. reporting framework has long been viewed as a global benchmark for transparency and investor protection. A move away from quarterly reporting could represent a step backward relative to this standard and may negatively impact investor confidence in U.S. capital markets.

For these reasons, I believe the current quarterly reporting framework strikes an appropriate balance between transparency and compliance burden and should be retained. Any efforts to reduce regulatory burden would be better directed toward simplifying disclosures, modernizing reporting requirements, or providing targeted relief for smaller issuers rather than reducing the frequency of financial reporting.

271:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Thomas Bourveau

Thomas Bourveau
Saïd Business School, University of Oxford
Park End Street, Oxford, OX1 1HP

May 17, 2026

Re: Proposal to Allow Optional Semiannual Reporting - File S7-2026-15

Dear Chairman Atkins,

I submit this letter in response to the Commission proposed amendments to Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13, which would permit issuers registered with the SEC to elect to file semiannual reports on new Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q. I am a Professor of Accounting at the University of Oxford. I write as a scholar who has extensively studied mandatory and voluntary disclosure and auditing in U.S. capital markets. In this letter, I offer insights drawn from academic research to help inform the economic analysis of the proposed amendment.

Unlike many comments in the public debate, the question at stake is not whether investors in public companies should receive financial statements every quarter, but rather whether the Commission should impose a fixed reporting frequency on issuers. There is a longstanding scholarly debate concerning the necessity of mandatory disclosure in capital markets. Although this debate extends beyond the specific questions raised by the proposed amendment to the Exchange Act Rules, it remains relevant to the present inquiry. The central argument to justify a disclosure mandate is the presence of a market failure. Two types of market failure are often cited: (1) market forces may be insufficient to resolve the information asymmetry between public issuers and investors; and (2) information disclosure generates externalities, such that the amount of disclosure produced through market forces falls below the level that would be optimal for the market (or society) as a whole.

Below, I provide some observations about the limited evidence to support these two sources of market failure and discuss them in the context of the proposed amendments. I conclude my letter with some comments on the potential benefits of this proposed amendment to make quarterly reporting optional.

(1) Disclosure mandates are necessary to resolve the information asymmetry between the issuers and their investors so the Commission should maintain the universal quarterly reporting frequency for issuers registered with the SEC.

This argument rests on two pillars: (i) insiders possess private information about a company's economic performance that is relevant to investors; and (ii) insiders fail to disclose this information voluntarily and in a timely manner to investors. While the first point is widely accepted, the second is far more debatable. In fact, several theoretical studies in information economics from the 1980s suggest that, under certain assumptions, economic agents will eventually reveal their private information (Grossman 1981; Milgrom 1981). The intuition is straightforward: if an agent withholds information, market participants may interpret this nondisclosure as a negative signal, leading them to revise their beliefs about the agent's prospects downward. This process leads all but the worst agents to disclose their information. This theory is difficult to test in the real world because the disclosure of financial statements has been mandated at the federal level for nearly a century in U.S. capital markets. Moreover, state-level "blue sky laws" predated federal regulation and often required financial disclosure from issuers seeking to sell securities to the public in order to protect investors. The pervasiveness of disclosure mandates in capital markets should not be interpreted as evidence that unregulated markets are unable to resolve information asymmetry between issuers and investors.

In a recent paper, my co-authors and I test whether information unravel in capital markets (Bourveau, Breuer & Stoumbos, "Learning to Disclose: Disclosure Dynamics in the 1890s Streetcar Industry," Review of Financial Studies 2025). To do so, we leverage the technological disruption caused by the electrification boom in the late 19th century that allowed the emergence of streetcar companies as the default urban transportation mode. Newly established streetcar companies required a large amount of capital to finance miles of tracks and cars, which created significant investment opportunities. To satisfy investor demand for information on these opportunities, business newspapers started issuing regular supplements dedicated to reporting company-specific information on almost all existing streetcar companies. Using a comprehensive sample of streetcar companies in the North East of the United States, we find that 72% of companies disclose their financial statements voluntarily in the first specialized supplement of a prominent business media, and that this rate increases rapidly to reach 95% within two years. Our findings suggest that an almost full disclosure equilibrium is reached over a short period of time.

Evidence from the 1890s streetcar industry shows that, even without mandatory disclosure requirements, public issuers facing investor demand voluntarily disclosed their financial performance. In other words, market forces can often resolve information asymmetry between issuers and investors through voluntary disclosure. Applied to the current policy debate, these findings suggest that if quarterly reporting were made optional, issuers would likely continue to provide quarterly earnings information if investors demand it, arguing that it constitutes first-order sources of information for their capital allocation decisions.

(2) Earnings information generate externalities in capital markets so the Commission should maintain quarterly reporting for all SEC-registered issuers

The argument holds that market forces lead companies to provide investors with the optimal amount of information on an individual basis. However, the total amount of disclosure produced by market forces may be insufficient, because information disclosure generates externalities that companies do not internalize. As a result, the overall level of financial disclosure is likely to be too low.

This argument appears quite weak for several reasons:

- It is admittedly very challenging to provide rigorous evidence that disclosure mandates correct a market failure due to the presence of market-wide externalities. However, studies based on European private companies fails to find evidence that reporting mandates unambiguously improve the efficiency of resource allocation in the economy (Breuer, "How Does Financial-Reporting Regulation Affect Industry-Wide Resource Allocation?," Journal of Accounting Research 2021).
- The market-wide information loss will be a function of the portion of issuers that will switch to semi-annual reporting along with their relative importance for systemic risk in capital markets. This information loss will likely remain limited:
  - Disclosure choices are known to be sticky, suggesting that most issuers will continue to file 10-Q filings or become hybrid reporters that provide some voluntary financial information during the first and third quarter.
  - Issuers that would choose to report semi-annually are likely those for whom the benefits of quarterly reporting are minimal. Capital market benefits tend to be lower for issuers operating mature businesses with stable economic performance, where more frequent updates provide little new information to market participants. As a result, the market-wide information loss from these issuers would likely be limited.
  - Issuers that would choose to report semi-annually are likely those for whom the costs of quarterly reporting are highest. Preparation costs are proportionally greater for smaller issuers, and the opportunity costs of preparing reports are higher for high-growth companies. However, smaller high-growth issuers represent only a small portion of the value-weighted capital market, further limiting any potential market-wide information loss.

(3) The potential benefits of a flexible reporting regime

I would like to highlight two potential benefits from the proposed amendment to make quarterly reporting optional:

- Carefully quantifying the compliance costs of reporting and auditing regulations for SEC-registered public issuers has proven challenging. By introducing a flexible reporting regime, the Commission would allow issuers to reveal their preferences, making it possible to observe how many view the switch to semi-annual reporting as beneficial (i.e., when the costs of quarterly reporting outweigh the benefits). Tracking the number of issuers that switch and analyzing their economic characteristics could provide valuable insights for the Commission in considering further efforts to simplify the reporting regime for SEC registrants.
- The Commission must balance investor protection with the goal of facilitating capital formation. The economic analysis of the proposed amendment indicates that, relative to the overall size of U.S. capital markets, the aggregate cost savings from reduced reporting frequency would be modest, suggesting only a limited impact on capital formation at the market level. However, these cost savings could be much more significant for smaller, high-growth issuers, for whom compliance costs represent a larger burden. Allowing such firms to opt out of quarterly reporting may enable them to better focus on their core business activities. A reduction in reporting frequency for some issuers does not necessarily mean diminished investor protection. Investor protection is achieved through both reporting requirements and the SEC's direct oversight activities. To maintain robust investor safeguards while easing the burden on issuers for whom quarterly reporting is especially costly, the Commission could reallocate its oversight resources to focus more closely on those issuers that choose to report less frequently. In other words, the Commission could incorporate issuers' chosen reporting frequency into its risk-based oversight framework, ensuring that limited resources are targeted where the potential risks to investors are greatest. (This point echoes recent findings from Chinese capital markets: Bourveau, Gao, Li & Zhou, "Comply-or-explain regulation and investor protection," Journal of Accounting and Economics 2025.)

Sincerely,
Thomas Bourveau

275:
Subject: Public Comment on File No. S7-2026-15 — Opposition to Semi-Annual Reporting Transition

To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am writing as a self-directed retail investor and software developer who builds custom analytical tools for portfolio management. I strongly oppose the proposal to shift from quarterly (10-Q) to semi-annual (6-month) reporting requirements.

Moving to a semi-annual schedule does not "modernize" our markets. Instead, it obscures them. Specifically, I would like the Commission to consider the following points:

- Institutional Information Asymmetry: In a 2026 market defined by high-frequency trading and real-time data, a six-month reporting gap is an eternity. Large institutional firms have the resources to "proxy" a company's health through alternative data methods such as satellite imagery, credit card scrapes, and supply chain tracking, et. al. The individual investor relies on the SEC's periodic filings as the only verified "level playing field." Removing two of these checks per year hands a permanent, unfair advantage to institutions and hedge funds.

- The "Cost Savings" Fallacy: The argument that this change saves significant capital for issuers is mathematically flawed. These companies maintain the same accounting systems, the same internal audit teams, and the same compliance staff regardless of filing frequency. The cost of "preparing the materials" is a decimal point on the balance sheet of a mid-to-large cap firm. Trading market transparency for negligible administrative savings is a poor bargain for the American retail investor.

- The Risk of "Information Shocks": When reporting is frequent, the market can digest data in smaller increments. Moving to 180-day windows will lead to massive, destabilizing "gap-downs" or "gap-ups" when the reports finally drop. For those of us who manage risk using a 13-week performance window, a six-month vacuum makes disciplined risk management nearly impossible.

I urge the Commission to maintain the current quarterly reporting standards. Transparency should not be sacrificed for the sake of corporate convenience.

Sincerely,
/s/
Christopher G. Kearns
RigidBody Tech LLC

279:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: James Parker

Re: S7-2026-15. Dear Ms. Countryman,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commission’s proposal to transition mandatory financial reporting for public companies from a quarterly (3-month) to a semiannual (6-month) cadence. While the stated goal of this change is to reduce "short- termism" and compliance costs, the proposal fails to account for the severe informational disadvantages it imposes on retail investors and the broader risks to market stability. My opposition is based on the following points.

First, this rule change ignores the fundamental structural gap between institutional and retail investors. Small investors rely heavily on standardized, frequent quarterly reports because, unlike large institutional firms, they lack direct access to senior management. Without the 90-day pulse of Form 10-Q filings, retail investors are essentially locked out of the information loop for half the year, while large funds continue to leverage their size to gain private insights.

Second, reducing the frequency of reporting provides management with an unacceptable window to delay the disclosure of negative material news. The history of the markets shows that when companies are allowed longer gaps between public filings, bad news is often buried until it can no longer be hidden, resulting in "information dumps" that trigger extreme price volatility. This undermines the SEC’s mission to maintain orderly markets.

Third, the Commission has not adequately addressed the unknown impact this change will have on derivative markets, particularly the options market. Options are vital tools for investors seeking to hedge their positions and manage risk. Option pricing and liquidity are deeply tied to the regular arrival of quarterly financial data. Extending the reporting cycle could drastically increase the cost of hedging and reduce liquidity in these essential markets.

Furthermore, I am deeply concerned that a 6-month cycle will exacerbate the exchange of material non-public information. Management frequently holds private meetings with large investors and sell-side analysts between public reporting periods. Doubling the time between public disclosures creates a dangerous six-month "blackout" for the public, during which management can provide private "color" or guidance to favored parties. This creates an environment of asymmetrical information where retail investors are systematically traded against by those with better access.

In conclusion, the proposed shift to semiannual reporting prioritizes corporate convenience over investor protection. It erodes transparency, invites market volatility, and tilts the playing field even further toward institutional players. I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain the quarterly reporting standards that have made U.S. capital markets the most transparent in the world.

Sincerely,

Sincerely,
James A Parker
Charlotte, North Carolina

Dear Members of the Commission,

I unequivocally oppose any proposal to weaken mandatory quarterly reporting by replacing it with semiannual reporting. Such a change would weaken the U.S. disclosure regime, undermining investor protection, market transparency, and corporate accountability. Timely, recurring financial reporting is a cornerstone of capital market integrity-not an administrative burden.

As a professor of accounting holding bachelorâs, masterâs, and doctoral degrees with a concentration in accounting, and as a Certified Fraud Examiner with experience in financial reporting, forensic accounting, and corporate governance, I believe this proposal would materially increase systemic risk. It would impair the ability of investors and regulators to detect performance deterioration, liquidity stress, and fraudulent activity in a timely manner.

Quarterly reporting ensures that capital allocation decisions are based on current, reliable information. Extending reporting intervals to six months would expand informational blind spots, heighten information asymmetry, and allow misstatements and governance failures to persist longer before detection.

From a fraud and governance perspective, reduced reporting frequency weakens scrutiny, diminishes deterrence, and increases the opportunity to conceal adverse financial conditions.

 Reducing transparency does not ease reporting pressure-it simply delays when problems are uncovered and allows the consequences to become more severe.

Public companies benefit from access to capital markets; in return, they owe timely and transparent disclosure. Weakening this obligation shifts risk to investors and erodes trust in market integrity.

The SECâs mandate is to protect investors and ensure fair, efficient markets. Semiannual reporting is incompatible with that mandate.

For these reasons, the Commission must reject this proposal. Any move to reduce reporting frequency would be a step backward for U.S. capital markets-weakening transparency, diminishing accountability, and undermining investor trust.

Respectfully submitted,

Dr. Fahim Mazumdar, DBA, MSA, CFE

289:
Subject: Comments on proposal for Form 10-S (File No. S7-2026-15)
From: Marshal Shichtman, Esq., MBA, LLM — Marshal Shichtman & Associates, P.C.

I would like to make a few comments on the proposal for proposed Form 10-S.

In an age when everything is moving faster, I am stunned that the Commission is proposing rules to slow down the process. We have virtually real time trading and next day settlements. Today’s market operates practically in real time. However, effectively all transactions are premised upon information. Information the issuer disseminates primarily. I fail to see how that makes the markets more efficient, more orderly, or builds investor confidence. Those are the foundational items upon which the American capital markets are built and upon which it’s reputation stands. I believe this proposal may have the opposite effect. Moreover, it gives foreign markets a competitive advantage, at a time when new U.S. issuers are at a low point. That is the overview.

I am skeptical that issuers will see an aggregate material savings by reducing two Form 10-Qs. All the PCAOB auditors are still going to go through the same amount of material for the year. However, they have two less looks by not reviewing the Form 10-Qs allowing items to take root or fester. It is the old proverb of an ounce of prevention is equal to a pound of cure. Or in modern parlance, maintenance is cheaper than breakdowns.

One of the subline benefits of all issuers doing quarterly reports, is all issuers are doing quarterly reports. The uniformity creates an apples-to-apples effect. It is not simply an issue of dividing by two, it is the regularity of the information provided. All issuers reporting quarterly creates a momentum of consumer confidence.

I also have concerns with market mechanics and collateral effects. Quarterly reporting is baked into a variety of market mechanics such as: provisions in financing agreements for defaults based upon regular reporting (and I have doubts that creditors are going to enjoy getting financial information less regularly, which creates an asymmetric non- public information issue); Rule 144 adequate public information (although entitlement holders are availing themselves less frequently of Rule 144 exemptions, I believe the

Commission will face definitional encounters with the timeliness of adequate public information); delayed listing determinations (e.g. shareholders equity for minimum listing or delisting determinations), etc. The more you look at the market mechanics the more potential issues you see. Admittedly, it probably is not insurmountable, but you can also drive a car on three wheels. I do not see the cost benefit of making life easier for American Issuers at the expense of operating American markets on three wheels.

Lastly, I would respectfully request that the Commission reduce the size of their own proposals. This proposal was 279 pages. I sincerely doubt everyone read the whole proposal, let alone could digest the 279 pages. I fully understand the need to be thorough and the magnitude of the Commission’s proposals that will have a systemic effect on orderly markets, investor confidence, and valuations. However, the sheer volume of pages generated makes things less clear rather than more clear due to the indigestibility of the magnitude of pages. Please consider reducing the page count.

Yours, etc., Marshal Shichtman, Esq.

From: Mayra Rodriguez Valladares

I respectfully submit my comments about the semiannual reporting proposal with a focus on banks. https : / / www . forbes . com / sites / mayrarodriguezvalladares / 2026 / 05 / 06 / the-hidden-risk-of-less-disclosure-why-banks-need-quarterly-reporting /

Best regards,
Mayra Rodriguez Valladares

---

## SUMMARY OF THE LINKED FORBES ARTICLE (summarized for the tracker; not the original text)

Her SEC comment links to her Forbes article "The Hidden Risk of Less Disclosure: Why Banks Need Quarterly Reporting" (2026-05-06). In my own words:

She opposes the optional-semiannual proposal as applied to banks. Banking runs on confidence, so anything that increases opacity is dangerous. The largest banks will stay quarterly; the consequential cohort is mid-sized regional banks (roughly $20-150B in assets), which face real cost pressure and thinner analyst coverage and are the most likely to opt in, and that is exactly where transparency is already most fragile. Under semiannual reporting, gradual credit deterioration and deposit outflows stay invisible until they surface all at once, turning what would have been incremental repricing into a single shock; in a digitally connected system (the 2023 SVB collapse is her reference point) that can trigger rapid deposit flight and contagion to peer institutions, especially other semiannual reporters that investors cannot easily distinguish. She also flags a "signaling trap": once some banks switch, the choice itself signals lower transparency or higher risk, so stronger banks keep quarterly while switchers face higher funding costs, stratifying the system. She urges regulators, investors, credit-rating analysts, and banks to file comments.
296:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Hayden Ramsey

I am writing in support of the Commission's proposed optional semiannual reporting framework. I believe the proposal represents a thoughtful modernization of the public company reporting system and could meaningfully improve operational efficiency, reduce unnecessary compliance burdens, and encourage additional companies to access the public markets.

First, the current quarterly reporting structure imposes substantial administrative and financial burdens on public companies, particularly smaller issuers and emerging growth companies. Public company finance and accounting teams spend significant time each quarter preparing Form 10-Q filings, coordinating external auditors and legal counsel, drafting disclosures, reviewing internal controls, and responding to evolving regulatory expectations. While transparency remains critically important, much of this process is repetitive and resource-intensive relative to the incremental informational value provided every ninety days.

Allowing companies the option to report semiannually would create meaningful efficiencies by enabling management teams to devote more time toward long-term operational execution, strategic planning, capital allocation, and innovation rather than continuous short-cycle reporting preparation. This would be especially impactful for smaller public companies that often operate with lean finance organizations and disproportionately high compliance costs relative to their market capitalization.

Second, the proposal could help reduce barriers to entry for private companies considering becoming publicly listed. The cost and complexity associated with maintaining public company reporting requirements is frequently cited as a deterrent to entering the public markets. Quarterly reporting requirements contribute significantly to those costs through legal fees, accounting fees, audit review procedures, disclosure preparation, investor relations support, and internal staffing demands.

An optional semiannual framework could make public market participation more accessible to growth-stage companies by lowering recurring compliance expenditures and reducing the operational strain associated with quarterly reporting cycles. Over time, this may encourage a broader and healthier public company ecosystem by making the public markets more attractive to innovative companies that might otherwise remain private.

Third, the proposal recognizes that modern markets already operate within an environment of continuous disclosure. Material developments would still require timely reporting through Form 8-K filings, earnings releases, investor presentations, and other public communications. As a result, investors would continue to receive prompt disclosure regarding material events while companies gain flexibility around formal periodic reporting requirements.

Importantly, the proposal is optional rather than mandatory. This preserves market-driven decision-making by allowing companies and investors to determine whether quarterly or semiannual reporting best fits a company's specific circumstances, investor base, and industry dynamics. Companies that believe quarterly reporting provides competitive advantages or better aligns with shareholder expectations would remain free to continue that practice.

In my view, the proposal appropriately balances investor protection with regulatory efficiency and reflects an overdue reassessment of whether the current quarterly reporting structure remains optimally calibrated for today's public markets.

Thank you for considering these comments.

To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am writing to oppose the Commission's proposed rule permitting public companies to replace quarterly Form 10-Q reporting with semiannual reporting on new Form 10-S.
I understand the stated goal of reducing compliance burdens and giving companies more flexibility. However, the public markets do not exist only for the convenience of reporting companies. They exist because investors, employees, retirees, pension funds, analysts, and the broader public can rely on timely, standardized, and comparable information. Reducing mandatory reporting from quarterly to semiannual would weaken that foundation.

Quarterly reporting is not just an administrative exercise. It is one of the core transparency mechanisms that allows investors to evaluate company performance, monitor risk, compare companies, and detect changes before they become larger problems. Moving to semiannual reporting would create longer periods where material business trends, financial deterioration, operational risks, liquidity concerns, or governance issues may remain less visible to the public.

This proposal would also increase information asymmetry. Large institutional investors, insiders, sophisticated analysts, and market participants with access to alternative data may still find ways to monitor company performance between reports. Ordinary investors will not have the same access. A system that gives public companies less frequent disclosure obligations risks widening the gap between those with privileged or expensive information channels and those relying on public filings.

I am also concerned that reducing reporting frequency could increase the risk of insider trading or perceived unfairness in the market. When companies go six months between required interim reports, insiders and select market participants may have access to meaningful performance information long before the public does. Even if existing rules still require disclosure of certain material events, semiannual reporting would reduce the regular cadence of structured, comprehensive financial disclosure.

The proposal also raises concerns about comparability. If some companies continue quarterly reporting while others move to semiannual reporting, investors may lose a consistent basis for comparing companies across industries. Optional reporting flexibility may sound reasonable, but in practice it could create a fragmented disclosure landscape where companies with weaker performance have an incentive to disclose less frequently.

The Commission's mission includes protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation. This proposal may reduce costs for some registrants, but those savings should not come at the expense of transparency, investor protection, market confidence, and fair access to information. For these reasons, I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed semiannual reporting option. Public companies that access U.S. capital markets should continue to provide quarterly Form 10-Q reports as a baseline obligation of public ownership.

Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Arnold Bass

I am writing as an individual investor to express my strong opposition to any proposal that would reduce the frequency of corporate earnings reporting from quarterly to twice a year. I believe such a change would meaningfully weaken market transparency, reduce investor confidence, and ultimately harm both retail investors and the broader financial system.

Quarterly reporting is one of the most important mechanisms we have for maintaining timely, accurate insight into a companyâs financial health. These reports allow investors to evaluate performance trends, assess management decisions, and make informed investment choices based on current information rather than outdated data. Moving to semiannual reporting would create longer periods of uncertainty, widen information gaps, and increase the likelihood of market volatility driven by speculation rather than facts.

Less frequent reporting would also disadvantage individual investors compared to institutional investors who have greater access to alternative data sources. Quarterly disclosures help level the playing field by ensuring that all market participants receive standardized, regulated information at predictable intervals.

Furthermore, quarterly reporting promotes accountability. Companies are more likely to maintain disciplined financial practices when they know their performance will be reviewed and disclosed every three months. Reducing this cadence risks weakening oversight and could allow emerging problems to go unnoticed for longer periods.

While I understand the desire to reduce regulatory burdens on public companies, I believe the benefits of quarterly reporting far outweigh the costs. Transparency is the foundation of healthy markets, and any step that reduces it would be a step backward for investors and the public.

Thank you for considering my perspective on this important issue. I urge the Commission to preserve quarterly earnings reporting requirements in the interest of fairness, transparency, and market stability.

To Whom It May Concern at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission:
I am writing as an individual retail investor to express my strong opposition to any proposed rule that would reduce the frequency of public company financial reporting from quarterly to semi-annual.

More frequent disclosure discourages earnings manipulation, provides earlier warning of financial distress, and gives investors the data needed to hold company leadership responsible for stated strategies and guidance.

38:
Public Comment on Proposed Rule S7-2026-15 - Semiannual Reporting

To the Securities and Exchange Commission,

I am submitting this comment in opposition to the proposed amendments under File Number S7-2026-15, which would allow public companies to file semiannual reports in lieu of quarterly reports.

I am a software developer building financial transparency infrastructure that tracks institutional investment activity using public SEC filings, on-chain blockchain data, and real-time disclosure feeds. My perspective is technical and practical: the cost savings argument underpinning this proposal is obsolete, and the proposal moves in the wrong direction at precisely the wrong moment.

The compliance cost burden cited in support of this rule assumes a world of manual accounting, physical filings, and costly reconciliation processes. That world no longer exists for the vast majority of public companies. Modern financial systems are digital, automated, and continuously maintained. The marginal cost of packaging already-maintained books into a quarterly filing is minimal. The heavy lifting -auditing, internal controls, financial statement preparation - happens on a rolling basis regardless of reporting frequency. Reducing filings from four to two per year does not meaningfully reduce that burden.

More importantly, this proposal ignores where financial markets are heading. Blockchain-based financial infrastructure - already live and operational on networks including XRP Ledger and Stellar - enables continuous, real-time, permissionless disclosure. Institutional transactions are already being recorded on public ledgers with millisecond timestamps. The trajectory of modern capital markets is toward greater transparency and higher frequency disclosure, not less. Proposing to halve reporting frequency in 2026 is a step backward relative to where the technology already is.

Reducing disclosure frequency does not harm all market participants equally. Institutional insiders, large asset managers, and company executives maintain continuous access to operational data regardless of what is filed publicly. Retail investors and independent analysts depend entirely on public filings. Extending the blind spot from 90 days to 180 days widens an already significant information asymmetry - and does so in a way that primarily benefits those who already have the least need for public disclosures.

Academic research supports this concern. Studies of European markets, which moved to semiannual reporting before partially reversing course, found that reduced reporting frequency created information vacuums that caused investors to overreact to peer-firm disclosures and produced more volatile, less efficient stock prices. The U.S. should not repeat that experiment.

I urge the Commission to reject this proposal and instead direct its attention toward modernizing disclosure infrastructure - leveraging existing and emerging technology to make reporting more frequent, more accessible, and more useful to all market participants, not less.

Respectfully submitted,
[Morten Hansen]
[Tampere, Finland]

39:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am submitting this comment regarding Release No. 2026-42, "SEC Proposes Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies."

The proposal raises an important question beyond the commonly discussed tradeoff between regulatory flexibility and disclosure frequency. It also raises a market-function question: what happens when the interval between verified financial reporting "reality checks" expands from three months to six months?

Quarterly reporting does not eliminate uncertainty, nor does it prevent markets from relying on estimates, guidance, sell-side models, or alternative data. But it does compress the period during which management assumptions, consensus expectations, and market narratives can drift materially from underlying operating conditions before being reset by standardized financial disclosure.

If public companies are permitted to elect semiannual reporting, investors may place greater reliance on interim guidance, unaudited narratives, alternative data, and model-driven estimates between filings. This may be especially important in capital-intensive sectors where operating risk can form before it appears cleanly in financial statements, including utilities, energy infrastructure, AI infrastructure, data centers, advanced manufacturing, transportation, and other long-lived asset sectors.

In these sectors, material developments often emerge through changes in operating assumptions: demand durability, utilization, construction timelines, project cost escalation, customer commitments, supply-chain constraints, rate recovery expectations, and capital-allocation decisions. Less frequent standardized reporting could increase the duration over which such assumptions remain unverified by formal financial disclosure.

For that reason, I respectfully encourage the Commission to evaluate not only whether optional semiannual reporting reduces compliance burden, but also whether longer reporting intervals may increase market reliance on non-standardized operating signals between filings.

The relevant issue is not simply whether quarterly or semiannual reporting is preferable. It is whether investors retain sufficient decision-grade information to distinguish durable operating performance from assumption-driven performance during longer intervals between required reports.

If the Commission proceeds with an optional semiannual reporting framework, it should consider whether additional safeguards, disclosure expectations, or sector-sensitive guidance may be appropriate where long-lived capital commitments, major infrastructure programs, large customer concentration, or material demand assumptions are central to investor valuation.

Respectfully submitted,
Neil P. Osnato

40:
Comment Statement to the Securities and Exchange Commission
Re: Proposed Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies
Release No. 2026-42

Dear Commissioners of the SEC,

I am writing to formally express my opposition to the Commission's proposal (Release No. 2026-42) to permit public companies the option of semiannual reporting via Form 10-S. While I recognize the Commission's intent to alleviate regulatory burdens and mitigate "short-termism," this proposal represents a significant retreat from the transparency and data-frequency standards that have historically made the U.S. capital markets the most trusted and liquid in the world.

My opposition is based on the following primary concerns:

**1. Erosion of Transparency and Price Discovery**
The quarterly reporting cycle (Form 10-Q) provides the essential "heartbeat" of market information. Moving to a six-month reporting interval would create massive informational "dead zones," leading to increased market volatility as investors are forced to rely on speculation rather than verified financial data for half the year. This degradation of information quality will inevitably impair efficient price discovery.

**2. Information Asymmetry and Insider Risk**
Extended reporting gaps provide a structural advantage to insiders and institutional entities with direct access to management, to the profound detriment of retail investors. Widening the window between public disclosures increases the risk of information leakage and heightens the potential for insider trading, undermining the SEC's core mission of investor protection.

**3. Technological Obsolescence of the "Regulatory Burden" Argument**
The proposal's justification — that quarterly reporting is an undue administrative burden — fails to account for the current technological landscape. In 2026, the widespread adoption of Generative AI, automated XBRL integration, and real-time ledger systems has fundamentally transformed financial reporting.
- Reduced Compliance Costs: AI-driven financial reporting tools have significantly lowered the marginal cost and labor required to produce 10-Q filings.
- Modernized Oversight: If a public company possesses the digital infrastructure to compete in the modern market, it possesses the capability to provide quarterly snapshots. To suggest otherwise ignores the efficiencies gained through contemporary enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems.

**4. Market Fragmentation and Comparability Issues**
An "optional" reporting framework will lead to a bifurcated market. Investors will struggle to compare a quarterly-reporting company against a semiannual competitor. This lack of standardization will likely result in a "transparency discount" for semiannual filers, potentially increasing their cost of capital — the exact opposite of the proposal's intended effect.

**5. Failure to Address "Short-Termism"**
There is no empirical evidence that reducing the frequency of financial reporting will cause management to prioritize long-term growth. True long-termism is driven by corporate culture and executive compensation structures, not by withholding material financial information from the public for six months at a time.

**Conclusion**
At a time when technology allows for more transparency, not less, the SEC should be leveraging modern technologies to make disclosures more accessible and timely, rather than permitting companies to retreat into a six-month silence. I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain the quarterly reporting standards that ensure a level playing field for all market participants.

Sincerely,
Omer Yousif, PhD

Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Walter Sysak

I am writing as a retail investor to oppose the proposal allowing companies to file semiannual reports on Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q.
Markets move faster every year, not slower. Cutting public reporting in half does not reduce the information a company generates. Books will still be closed monthly. This only widens the knowledge gap between insiders and retail investors. Disclosure rules exist to narrow that gap.
Longer gaps between filings also concentrate price-moving information into fewer events, resulting in increased volatility. Retail investors, who depend almost entirely on public filings, will bear the brunt of that volatility.
The Commission's investor-protection mandate should weigh more heavily than the filing-cost relief this proposal offers. To preserve market stability and the confidence of everyday investors, I urge the Commission to maintain the mandatory quarterly reporting schedule.

424:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: John Hancock

To the SEC Commissioners:

I am an attorney employed by a large insurance company and asset management firm. Our organization sponsors offerings registered on Forms S-1, S-3, N-1A, N-2, and N-4.

Please accept the following comments regarding the proposal to transition from quarterly Exchange Act reports to semiannual reporting.

While I recognize that certain reporting obligations impose unnecessary burdens on issuers, I do not believe that reducing the frequency of transparency is the appropriate solution. Instead, I recommend requiring issuers to prepare their management discussion and analysis and provide key performance indicators, including earnings, on a quarterly basis. More comprehensive disclosures could then be provided on a semi-annual schedule. This approach would reduce burdens while preserving timely insight for investors.

I would also like to address the broader discussion around revitalizing the initial public offering market. Although efforts to reduce regulatory burdens are helpful, the most impactful step to make IPOs more attractive would be to raise the accredited investor standard. Because inflation has significantly eroded the value of that threshold over more than forty years, there is now ample private capital available. As a result, many issuers no longer need to access the public markets. Additionally, permitting retail oriented investment funds to hold substantial private assets further reduces the necessity of going public. Today, companies are driven to pursue IPOs primarily by elevated valuations fueled by the scarcity of offerings and favorable market conditions and mostly as a windfall to those private market investors.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,
John Hancock

P.S. I am not affiliated with John Hancock/ManuLife.
