428:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Ravi Shah

I am writing to oppose the Commissionâs proposal to allow public companies to replace quarterly reporting on Form 10-Q with semiannual reporting on new Form 10-S. I respectfully urge the Commission to retain mandatory quarterly reporting for public companies.

The U.S. public markets are the deepest and most trusted in the world largely because investors receive regular, standardized, comparable, and reliable information. Quarterly reporting is not just a compliance exercise but a core component of market transparency that allows investors, analysts, lenders, employees, regulators, and the public to evaluate business performance in a timely and consistent manner.

The proposed semiannual option would reduce transparency at the exact moments when investors most need information. Reporting on a semiannual basis is too long in modern markets. Business conditions can change rapidly because of interest rates, tariffs, supply-chain disruptions, consumer weakness, credit conditions, litigation, cybersecurity incidents, accounting issues, customer concentration, or management execution problems. Investors should not have to wait half a year to receive standardized financial statements and management discussion from companies whose securities trade daily in public markets.

The proposal also risks creating an uneven information environment. If some companies continue to report quarterly while others report semiannually, investors will have less ability to compare peer companies during the year. A company reporting only twice annually could appear less volatile or less scrutinized simply because less information is available. That does not make the company stronger; it only makes the market less informed. The SECâs own proposing release recognizes this comparability concern by asking whether investors can reasonably compare a quarterly filer to a semiannual filer when first- or third-quarter information is not available.  

A voluntary semiannual regime may also disadvantage retail investors. Large institutions, insiders, sophisticated funds, private lenders, and well-connected analysts may still obtain information through management access, industry data, alternative data, private conversations, or selective corporate updates. Ordinary investors are more dependent on formal SEC filings. Reducing required reporting could widen the information gap between professional and retail market participants.

The likely substitute for quarterly reports would not be silence; it would be less standardized disclosure. Companies would still face market pressure to provide updates, issue press releases, hold investor calls, or selectively discuss business trends. But those communications may not provide the same level of consistency, liability discipline, financial-statement structure, MD&A, risk-factor updates, or comparability as Form 10-Q. In practice, the proposal could shift the market from standardized reporting to more fragmented, company-controlled messaging.

I understand the concern that quarterly reporting may contribute to short-term thinking. But eliminating or weakening quarterly reporting is not the right solution. The better distinction is between quarterly reporting and quarterly earnings guidance. The SEC can discourage excessive short-term earnings guidance without reducing the required financial disclosures that investors rely on. Long-term business planning and quarterly transparency are not mutually exclusive. A well-run company should be able to pursue long-term strategy while still providing investors with timely quarterly financial information.

The compliance cost argument should also be weighed carefully. Public companies receive significant benefits from access to public capital markets, including liquidity, valuation, credibility, and broad investor participation. In return, they should meet a baseline level of public transparency. For many investors, quarterly reporting is part of the bargain of investing in public securities rather than private businesses.

If the Commission is concerned about smaller companies or newly public companies, it could consider targeted relief rather than a broad semiannual option available to all reporting companies. For example, the SEC could simplify certain disclosure requirements, reduce duplicative items, or provide scaled reporting relief for smaller issuers while preserving the quarterly cadence. That would address compliance burdens without materially weakening market transparency.

For these reasons, I respectfully request that the Commission retain mandatory quarterly reporting on Form 10-Q. At minimum, if the Commission adopts any semiannual reporting option, it should be limited, carefully phased, and subject to investor-protection safeguards, including enhanced notice, shareholder approval or opt-out rights, clear risk disclosure, and restrictions for companies with elevated risk profiles.

Public markets work best when information is timely, standardized, comparable, and broadly available to all investors. Quarterly reporting supports those goals. Semiannual reporting would move the market in the wrong direction.

Thank you!

437:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Dave

I strongly oppose reducing public company reporting requirements from quarterly to semi-annual disclosures. Public markets function on transparency, accountability, and equal access to information. Reducing the frequency of required reporting weakens all three.

Quarterly reporting provides investors with regular insight into a company's financial condition, operational performance, risks, and management decisions. Moving from disclosures every ~90 days to every ~180 days would cut formal transparency in half and leave investors operating with significantly less verified information for longer periods of time.

This change would disproportionately harm individual investors. Large institutional firms can rely on analyst networks, proprietary research, and direct management access to fill information gaps. Retail investors depend far more heavily on public filings to make informed decisions. Fewer required disclosures would widen the informational advantage already held by institutions.

Reduced oversight also increases the risk of problems remaining hidden longer â including deteriorating finances, governance failures, aggressive accounting practices, or material business risks. Strong reporting standards are not merely administrative burdens; they are a core investor protection mechanism.

Public companies benefit enormously from access to public capital markets. In exchange, they should accept robust and frequent disclosure obligations. Accountability should increase with public ownership, not decrease.

If the concern is excessive short-term market pressure, the solution is not less transparency. The solution is encouraging long-term corporate governance while maintaining strong disclosure standards that protect investors and preserve confidence in public markets.

Most recently, the gains achieved on April occured thanks to Q1's reports being mostly positive after what seemed to be an uncertain time in March. If companies were given the option to perform semi-annual reporting, they'll go through the path of least resistance and leave investors in the dark as to their daily operations. A better option would be to provide enough incentive to perform quarterly, bimonthly or monthly reporting that the only companies that choose semi-annual reporting are the ones where they see redundancy compared to the benefits provided to them and the shareholders to performing quarterly reports.

Unless the world miraculously stabilized overnight, quarterly reporting keeps all investors up to date with the constant goings of the planet, which helps maintain premiums in stock market prices as any uncertainty is quelled by financial reporting.

Beyond the financial gains, I and other investors have found the quarterly reports quite useful and properly up-to-date. Usually, anything that is more than a few months old will lose its relevance and it will happen much sooner if something major happens like a war, or tariffs, or the many stints of the current administration that have affected business on more than just a quarterly basis. Institutionals and individual investors alike will not only benefit from quarterly reports, but from more frequent reporting the likes Progressive (PGR) have achieved with their monthly updates. There have also been times when I've been able to make use of quarterly reports to avoid entire bankruptcies that would have happened months later due to how quickly some financials have deteriorated. Semi-annual reports put investors at greater risk of financial loss and may erode the trust and goodwill of the US stock market at a global level, as fraud can be more easily achieved with more lax reporting requirements.

44:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal to move from quarterly (Form 10-Q) to semiannual (Form 10-S) reporting. While the stated goal is to reduce "short-termism," the actual result will be a dangerous reduction in market transparency that disproportionately harms retail investors. The current quarterly structure is the "gold standard" for a reason. Extending the reporting gap to six months creates a massive "dark period" that gives an unfair advantage to institutional firms and insiders. Large hedge funds and advanced firms already use alternative data such as satellite tracking of retail parking lots, private shipping manifests, and high-frequency credit card data to predict company performance in real time. Retail investors do not have access to these expensive tools; we rely on the 10-Q to level the playing field. By removing the quarterly requirement, the SEC is essentially ensuring that by the time a retail investor sees a semiannual report, the "news" is already six months old and has been fully traded upon by those with better technology. Furthermore, a 180 day gap between financial disclosures creates a breeding ground for insider trading. When material changes in a company's health are not disclosed for half a year, the temptation and opportunity for those with "inside" knowledge to front run the public grow exponentially. I urge the Commission to maintain the current quarterly reporting frequency to ensure the markets remain fair, transparent, and accessible to everyone, not just those with the most advanced tracking technology.

Sincerely,
Tan Banh

We have studied governance principles this semester and submit the following considerations for the Commission: 
1. Capital formation efficiency is based on trust. 461:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Charles D

I am vigorously opposed to the proposed Rule S7-2026-15 and any other proposal that would diminish in any way the sacred obligation of a publicly traded company to communicate with its investors on a regular basis, in good faith, with the utmost transparency. We investors rely heavily on frequent updates concerning the performance of our investments in order to make meaningful, timely decisions that impact the financial health of ourselves and our children. We have many bills to pay: rent or home mortgages, utilities, groceries, school tuition, clothing, transportation, taxes, to name but a few. As a retired physician I have no sympathy for the complaints of corporate compliance officers who decry the onerous burdens of regulatory obligations. Yes, they take time and extra effort, but they were put in place to ensure the safety of the public, especially from unscrupulous, malicious third parties. When critical decisions need to be made, more information is always better than less. During surgical procedures, the patient's vital signs are continuously monitored. Not once at the beginning and once at the end. As long as the trend of frequently reported data is acceptable, this frequency of reporting does not deter the surgeon from the initial objective or force the surgeon to concentrate on short-term goals. On the contrary, a constant awareness of the patient's condition, which if stable or improving, reassures the surgeon to continue the operation as planned and see it through to completion. What pilot would say, "I need to look at the altimeter and fuel gauge less."? A dead one. Patients, passengers, people, investors included, need to be protected by those whom we have entrusted to do it. Please do not abrogate your solemn duty to protect investors.

COMMENT LETTER
Re: File No. S7-2026-15 – Proposed Amendments Permitting Optional Semiannual
Reporting
To the Securities and Exchange Commission:
I appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule permitting optional
semiannual reporting for eligible public companies. I am attaching this amended comment
because I did not see an updated comment in relation to the new comment I sent yesterday
after the original one did not post correctly at the time. I made significant updates in the
meantime about when the public intent of the disclosure rules towards material weaknesses as
an early warning sign is not accomplished in practice due to lingering significant deficiencies
and late-changing classification as a significant deficiency.This comment responds specifically
to:
Request for Comment 18: “Would this practice create any new or heightened
investor protection concerns?”
and
Request for Comment 26: “For semiannual filers, what impact would a shift to
semiannual reporting have on: (1) companies’ disclosure controls and procedures,
(2) companies’ internal control over financial reporting, and (3) independent public
accountants’ strategy and approach for the annual audit of companies’ internal
control over financial reporting or financial statements? With semiannual reporting,
is there a potential for a material increase in the risk that material misstatements
(either due to error or fraud) or control deficiencies are not timely detected or
communicated to the independent public accountant thereby limiting potential
remediation of these issues by the issuer? Please provide any data related to these
questions.”
Introduction
There are conditions currently undisclosed to shareholders that would occur in semi-annual
reporting that based on my experience when present generate serious concern for a material
increase in the risk that material misstatements (either due to error or fraud) or control
deficiencies are not timely detected by or communicated to the independent public accountant

or misstatements, often accompanied with lingering significant deficiencies affecting other
material line items/metrics.
In quarterly reporting, emerging growth companies especially with the listed conditions present
benefit from additional feedback as the company continues to grow and from additional practice
performing quarterly close controls. More mature companies with the listed conditions present
benefit from more frequent operation of controls that brings with it increased precision.
Semiannual reporting alone will likely extend the timeline of ICFR control remediation in
period-end FR for some emerging growth companies (EGCs) over a greater number of years
and create lingering issues from material failures in ICFR from my limited experience. Hopefully,
increasing disclosure of these conditions will result in an incentive to improve capital markets
efficiency, governance, and SOX readiness.
A shift to semi-annual reporting under the current disclosure requirements for public companies
with now-undisclosed material revisions in total, material misstatements, or material ICFR

accompanied with lingering significant deficiencies materially affecting other material line
items/metrics used to value the company presents serious risks when there is a:
1) A compounding effect of material extraordinary risks to financial reporting from fraud or

reporting, occurring often alongside near-material failures in other ICFRs such as operational
controls for material line items/metrics used to value the company at the entity level. Sometimes
these controls are remediated in the last quarter (presenting planning/risk assessment
problems) or still classified as significant deficiencies at year-end without public disclosure of
steps taken to try to remediate them. Companies avoid public disclosure at all contrary to the
publicly listed intent of the rules.1 The effect of current disclosure practices results in companies

undisclosed revisions due to the current frequency and precision of account reconciliations,
management quarterly reviews, and inquiry from auditors as compared to semiannual reporting.
Semiannual reporting would give companies, particularly EGCs who might need it, much less
chances over time to put into practice corrective action that sometimes may take a couple years
to get correct. Quarterly reporting entails more frequent and more precise account
reconciliations that often help discover errors in practice, management reviews, and important
conversations quarterly about material balances that prevent surprises with unnecessary
costs/overruns at year end that are common in emerging growth companies with these
conditions present.
These risks are hard for retail investors to assess fully under the current disclosure
requirements, especially with the disclosures made in practice. Coupled with semiannual
reporting, the current disclosure requirements seem very inadequate to protect all investors’
interests. Especially for investors who may buy/sell the stock after the information is released
selectively if at all because as an investor it helps to find disclosure about these topics in an
Based on my experience, I do not think that the disclosure requirements following a material weakness in
financial reporting followed by widespread significant deficiencies in ICFR serve as an early warning
signal and problems with the current disclosure requirements would be made worse with a shift to
semiannual reporting due to the compounding nature of risk from recent period-end ICFR failures and
ICFR significant deficiencies affecting material line items/metrics like those in operational controls. Also a
longer lead time until corrective actions will be taken in practice due to less frequency and precision of
controls like account reconciliations and management reviews for some companies that adopt semiannual
reporting.
easy to compile XBRL format in a 10-K/Q/S to see a complete view of material information
management uses in making important decisions.
I think that narrowing the scope of who will be eligible for semiannual reporting to not include
public companies with these conditions present for at least three years after they are present
will increase capital markets efficiency and decrease the administrative burden on companies
with proper corporate reporting and governance priorities. Decreased comparability and
consistency in information provided by companies and in between years would be a negative
effect to information provided to investors as a result of these conditions.
Conditions That Should Prevent Semi-Annual Reporting
1. Revisions when in total material over a three year period because of the
compounding effect through quarters of most minor errors seen in practice. Minor
errors are often corrected before public disclosure is currently required after a few
attempts at financial reporting controls, like reconciliations, or through a greater
frequency and precision of review, including conversations, as the mistakes tend
to continue to grow larger over time.
Materiality should be considered over at least a three year rolling period to prevent
intentional one year shifts and one-off operational errors leading to an increased
administrative burden that is unproductive to protect investor interests. If revisions are
disclosed in total, as I think it should be, it should then be broken out to de minimis
materiality, just to say you should not be able to disclose separately determined material
components to certain investors in effect as is currently the case for disclosure made
about revisions like some presentation errors.
Not disclosing all de minimis components when total revisions are material would defeat
the principle of all investors including retail investors being provided decision useful
material information to avoid unforeseen risks that will adequately protect their interests.
Important to note important assumptions in de minimis materiality like a lack of detected
illegality and fraud. Also these disclosure should be made considering qualitative factors
like those listed in SEC Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 99 (SAB 99). Disclosures about the
scope of total revisions to retail investors are often not provided in practice since some
may be disclosed exclusively to institutional investors. Total quantitatively would be
decision-useful information provided to retail investors about the company's progress
toward improving corporate governance. Quantitative revisions should at least be
disclosed for all periods when it has forced the change to quarterly from semiannual
reporting in at least one of the comparative years.
Another benefit of this increased disclosure: It will easily allow retail investors to measure
progress towards remediation and achievement towards possible benefits associated
with semiannual reporting like reduced SG&A, instead of allowing companies to
selectively choose which presentation errors will look best together in a press release as
is achieved sometime under current disclosure requirements as conveniently-framed and
conveniently-timed reformation. Accordingly, all investors would be able to hold
management accountable for all decisions with more material information, including
shifts from quarterly to semiannual reporting as well as remediation steps, and whether
they match the investor’s risk profile. Especially when in practice revisions can be used
as an avenue for a convenient boost to qualitatively important short-term Non-GAAP
metrics, including those metrics important to some investors whose interests may not
adequately represent retail investors’.
2. Material Restatements: Important to mention with consideration of qualitative factors
like those listed in SEC Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 99 (SAB 99).
3. Material ICFR control failures in period-end financial reporting, occurring without
material revisions or restatements, and over the next three years near-material
ICFR failures, classified as significant deficiencies. These significant deficiencies
would have to materially affect material line items/metrics to be disclosed.
Undisclosed remedial action presents serious risks especially when near-material
ICFR failures are remediated in the fourth quarter without proper planning and risk
assessment or undisclosed remedial action leads to ongoing classification as a
significant deficiency at year end. Specifically for controls with
completeness/accuracy considerations like: operational controls that could
materially affect material line items/metrics used to value the company now and in
the future, especially in distress sales, bankruptcies, liquidations, or commodity
sell-offs.
Disclosure would also need to address steps taken to improve information technology.
Companies may not want to disclose control remediations affecting material line
items/metrics occurring in the fourth quarter since it could affect their stock price when
significant deficiencies and remediation methods could otherwise just be disclosed to
management and the audit committee only. However, material ICFR control remediation
in the last quarter of the year that is not subject to proper risk assessment including
around completeness/accuracy, walkthroughs, and feedback throughout the year or is
still classified as a significant deficiency at year end, can generally when combined with
recent failures in period end financial reporting present fraud and operational risks that
should be disclosed to shareholders. Both problems indicate semiannual reporting will
not adequately protect investor interests due to compounding effects of material fraud
and operational risk that could be prevented through more frequent and more precise
control operations like reconciliations, quarterly management reviews, and important
auditor inquiry with management about material balances. There is also usually a need
to increase staff after control failures like period-end financial reporting so an increased
administrative burden should not unduly burden companies that may require additional
staff over a medium term, and the company will likely benefit from additional feedback
throughout the year.
Conclusion on Conditions: These conditions contain information that is materially important to
retail investors, often undisclosed, and usually is accompanied with unforeseen results seen
over a length of time which should prevent semiannual reporting due to the nature of
compounding risk and longer lead times in semiannual reporting that as stated above will affect
(1) companies’ disclosure controls and procedures, (2) companies’ implementation and design
of their approach to remediating internal control over financial reporting, and (3) independent
public accountants’ strategy and approach for the annual audit of companies’ internal control
over financial reporting or financial statements
I would like to see conditions on the proposed semi-annual reporting rule or changes to the
disclosure requirements for public companies because it will:
● Increase risk assessment, planning, and feedback throughout the year around potential
ICFR material control failures through more frequent reviews. Possibly encouraging
companies to attempt to remediate their potential material failures in ICFRs early in the
year. Because not doing so until the end and disclosing the steps you took at the very
end that may have not been adequate, demonstrates the company's role in generating
significant waste through poor governance. Poor governance that would be seen initially
in ICFR material failures, next in disclosed remedial steps taken towards significant
deficiencies affecting material line items/metrics, and finally later in unforeseen costs for
investors that may appear.
● Increase disclosure around steps taken to remediate period-end ICFRs material failures
and near-material failures turned into significant deficiencies that may still materially
affect material line items/metrics such as those used to value the company, especially in
distress sales, bankruptcies, liquidations, or commodity sell-offs. Investors should be
given the information that would help them find answers to questions similar to
institutional investors in determining their risk profile. For example steps taken at each
business segment affected should be described if they are actually taken.
Practical Impact on Companies
Excluding companies that exhibit these risky characteristics from semiannual reporting is key to
address concerns that semi-annual reporting will make fraud or operational problems harder to
detect quickly based on my practical experience with following control failures and resulting
impacts to retail investors. Narrowing the rules will also provide additional incentives for
companies to avoid improperly taking advantage of these conditions that are common in
emerging growth companies and promote proper governance practices as well as increased
efficiency from improved incentives like that to improve SOX readiness. Robust implementation
of AI across companies may change the hiring needs of companies who face issues with the
conditions noted in this comment, but hiring at companies still usually does need to occur
regardless, to remediate the issues when these conditions exist. A question I think may take
more experience to answer is whether three years is appropriate to mandate a shift to quarterly
reporting from semi-annual for mature companies given the rate of change at companies with
the rate of AI adoption. Importantly, I think semi-annual reporting will probably extend the
timeline of remediation for emerging growth companies with material failures in ICFR in period
end in practice, along with the chance of associated errors in the meantime.
Companies will likely probably not want to disclose ICFR control failure remediation for
operational controls affecting material line items/metrics occurring in the last quarter of the year
or for near-material failures in ICFRs that are still classified as a significant deficiency at year
end because it could affect their stock price in their view unnecessarily. However, when
accompanied with period end financial reporting ICFR material failures that alone should
disqualify you from semiannual reporting in my view, the listed conditions present an additional
risk that should be disclosed to investors because of the nature of how information goes from
business segments and into the corporate consolidated financial statements.

470:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Christopher Craft

Here is a draft of the comment paragraphs for your SEC submission, written from the perspective of an active individual investor and financial literacy coach who relies heavily on quarterly data to maintain transparency and trust in the market.

---

### Why Individual Comments Matter to the SEC

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) explicitly states that public comments from individual investors are a vital part of the rulemaking process. While institutional investors have massive legal teams to voice their concerns, the SECâs primary mandate is to protect regular investors and maintain fair, orderly, and efficient markets. Hearing directly from individuals provides the Commission with real-world insights into how regulatory changes impact everyday market participants, ensuring that policy decisions are not shaped solely by corporate interests.

---

### Draft Comment for [Submit Comments on S7-2026-15](https : / / www . sec . gov / comments / s7-2026-15 / semiannual-reporting#no-back)

As a Sales Director, financial literacy coach, and active individual investor, I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal to shift from quarterly to semiannual reporting. In my role coaching others on financial literacy, I constantly emphasize that timely data is the bedrock of smart, confident investing. Personally, I rely extensively on Form 10-Q filings every single quarter to evaluate company performance, track operational health, and cross-reference financial statements against management's stated goals. Waiting six months for a single update severely diminishes an individual investor's ability to make agile, informed decisions.

Moving to a semiannual reporting structure significantly degrades corporate transparency and weakens our ability to keep tabs on C-suite behavior. A six-month window creates a dangerous information vacuum where corporate mismanagement, shifting risk profiles, or poor strategic decisions can be obscured for far too long. Reducing the frequency of these disclosures opens the door to unethical corporate behavior, as executives would face far less immediate accountability to the public.

Ultimately, eroding the frequency of these vital public filings will only breed widespread doubt in the integrity of the stock market. Transparency is what builds investor confidence; withholding information for longer periods will inevitably lead to market volatility and a erosion of trust. To protect the retail investing public and ensure market integrity, the SEC must maintain the current quarterly reporting framework.
471:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Ed. Brill

As an individual investor I believe for the SEC to reduce the frequency of public reporting requirements for businesses would be a huge step backward. I and other individual investors use those reports to make informed investment decisions. Investors need more and better information upon which to make investment decisions not less. If anything the frequency of today's quarterly reporting should be increased to every 2 months or even monthly.

Businesses have (or should have) the data for today's quarterly reporting available to them on a daily or weekly basis. Why not share it immediately with investors?

Why report twice a year? For which half years would be required? Spring and fall, i.e. first and last calendar halves? Or winter and summer? Many, perhaps most, manufacturing and service businesses have calendar cycles peculiar to each industry. Information on these seasonal variations is essential for making informed investment decisions.

Why not just once a year? Require annual reporting only without any interim reporting. At least information for calendar cyclic businesses would be directly comparable.

Better yet, let's go back a hundred years to the good old days, without reporting requirements and abolish the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) altogether and simply promote saving money "in a mattress"!

Sent from my iPhone
47:
I am submitting this comment in strong opposition to the proposed amendments that would allow public companies to file semiannual reports in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q.

The SEC's mandate is investor protection. This proposal moves in the opposite direction.
Reduced transparency increases investor risk. Quarterly reporting gives investors, analysts, and the market timely signals to price risk accurately.

Moving to semiannual reporting creates a six-month information vacuum. Academic research has documented that reduced reporting frequency leads investors to overreact to peer-company earnings, overcorrect when reports do arrive, and experience greater price volatility — not less. Less transparency does not produce more efficient markets. It produces less efficient ones.

The 2008 financial crisis is not ancient history. The systemic failures that nearly collapsed the global economy were enabled by opacity, inadequate disclosure, and insufficient oversight of corporate financial conditions. The regulatory response to that crisis was built on the premise that investors need more information, more frequently — not less. We have not resolved the structural vulnerabilities that made that crisis possible. Loosening disclosure requirements now is not reform. It is regression.

"Optional" frameworks erode in practice. Framing this as voluntary flexibility understates the real-world dynamics. When large-cap companies adopt semiannual reporting, smaller firms face pressure to follow to reduce compliance costs. What begins as an option becomes the floor. Investor protections are weakened for the entire market, not just the companies that elect the new cadence.

Compliance cost arguments do not justify the tradeoff. The burden-reduction case rests on the costs to companies. The SEC's obligation runs to investors, not issuers. Shifting that cost onto investors — in the form of information asymmetry, increased risk, and reduced ability to make informed decisions — is not a net regulatory benefit. It is a transfer of risk from corporations to the public.

The SEC should be strengthening oversight, not contracting it. Bad actors exist in public markets. They always have. Quarterly reporting does not prevent fraud, but it shortens the window in which fraud can go undetected. Extending that window by six months is a gift to those who exploit information gaps.

I urge the Commission to reject this proposal and reaffirm its commitment to the transparency standards that have distinguished U.S. capital markets for over five decades.

492:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Anthony Calime Jr.

I am an individual, self-directed investor who manages my own retirement and education investment accounts, including two 401(k) accounts and a 529 college savings account. I am concerned that allowing public companies to move from quarterly reporting to semiannual reporting would negatively affect my ability to make informed investment decisions.

As an individual investor, I rely on timely, standardized, and comparable company information to evaluate risks, track business performance, rebalance my accounts, and make responsible long-term investment choices. Reducing required reporting from quarterly to semiannual would create longer periods where investors like me have less reliable information about a companyâs financial condition.

Background
Quarterly reports help individual investors monitor company performance throughout the year. These reports provide important updates on revenue, earnings, cash flow, debt, margins, business risks, management discussion, and other financial information. While large institutions may have access to professional analysts, private research, management meetings, and advanced data tools, individual investors typically rely heavily on public filings.

My investment decisions affect long-term family goals, including retirement security and education funding. Because I self-manage my accounts, I need consistent and timely public information to evaluate whether companies remain suitable for my portfolio.

Semiannual reporting may reduce administrative burden for companies, but it may also reduce transparency for investors. A six-month reporting gap can allow material changes in business performance, debt levels, liquidity, competitive position, or operational risk to build up before ordinary investors receive a complete update.

Assessment
Moving to semiannual reporting would likely disadvantage individual investors in several ways.

First, it would reduce transparency. A lot can change in six months, especially in volatile sectors such as technology, banking, healthcare, energy, consumer discretionary, and small or mid-cap companies. Without quarterly reports, I would have fewer reliable checkpoints to assess whether a companyâs fundamentals are improving or deteriorating.

Second, it may increase information inequality. Large institutional investors often have more resources to interpret market signals, access expert research, and react quickly to partial information. Individual investors may be left relying on rumors, headlines, analyst opinions, or stock price movements instead of standardized public filings.

Third, it could increase market volatility. If investors receive fewer formal updates, each semiannual report could carry more surprise risk. That could lead to sharper stock price reactions when information is finally released, making it harder for individual investors to manage risk.

Fourth, it could make portfolio management harder. I use company information to decide whether to hold, buy, reduce, or sell positions across retirement and education accounts. Semiannual reporting would make it more difficult to identify changes in earnings quality, cash flow, debt, business outlook, and risk exposure in a timely manner.

Fifth, it could reduce accountability. Quarterly reporting creates regular discipline for management teams and boards. Less frequent reporting may make it easier for problems to remain hidden longer, which could harm shareholders who depend on timely disclosure.

I understand the concern that quarterly reporting can encourage short-term thinking. However, I believe the better solution is to discourage excessive short-term earnings guidance, not reduce the frequency of historical financial reporting. Investors need actual financial results more often than twice per year.

Recommendation
I recommend that regulators preserve quarterly reporting requirements for public companies. If semiannual reporting is allowed, it should include strong investor protections, such as:

Companies should still be required to provide timely quarterly financial updates or key performance data. 

Any company choosing semiannual reporting should clearly explain why the change benefits shareholders.

Companies should be required to disclose material changes promptly and in a format that is easy for retail investors to understand.

Investor protections should prioritize equal access to information for individual investors, not just institutions.

Regulators should distinguish between quarterly earnings guidance and quarterly financial reporting. Reducing unnecessary short-term guidance may be reasonable, but reducing historical financial reporting would harm transparency.

As a self-directed individual investor managing retirement and education accounts, I believe quarterly reporting is essential to fair, informed, and responsible investing. Moving to semiannual reporting would reduce the quality and timeliness of information available to ordinary investors and would make it harder for me to protect my familyâs long-term financial goals.

I respectfully oppose any efforts to loosen or eliminate quarterly reporting requirements for public companies. As an investor, I rely on timely, standardized disclosures to make informed decisions, assess risk, and monitor company performance. Quarterly reporting promotes transparency, market efficiency, and accountability by ensuring that material information is disclosed on a consistent and predictable basis.
Reducing the frequency or rigor of these reports would likely increase information asymmetry, disadvantage retail investors, and diminish confidence in public markets. While concerns about short-termism are valid, they can be addressed through improved disclosure practices and guidance rather than by reducing transparency.
Maintaining robust quarterly reporting standards is essential to preserving investor trust and the integrity of U.S. capital markets.

Even if SEC is persuaded a small group of investment fund companies and perhaps others anticipate this semiannual report model works for them, it could do a trial run of it for this group to see how stock markets react to it, and then roll it out broadly. But making the option available to the entire market without a careful experimental dry run carries more risks than rewards - less transparency, less inter/intra sector comparability, more potential for fraud, and malfeasance. These are concerns for the regulatory oversight and a trusted reporting system.

571:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Ryan Schaefer

Dear Secretary,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commissionâs proposal to allow public registrants the option to switch from a quarterly to a semi-annual reporting framework (Form 10-S). As an active market participant who relies on timely data for risk management and financial evaluation, I believe extending the disclosure cycle to six months will severely degrade the structural integrity, transparency, and efficiency of our capital markets.

My primary concerns regarding the transition to semi-annual reporting include:

Amplification of Gap Risk and Idiosyncratic Volatility: A six-month reporting cycle introduces a dangerous information vacuum. Under a semi-annual model, material changes in operational health, supply chain stability, and inventory velocity will remain obscured from the public for up to half a year. This lack of continuous disclosure will inevitably lead to massive, violent asset price gapping on earnings release dates. The market will see an increase in overnight capital destruction as investors are forced to absorb six months of accumulated operational surprises in a single trading session.

Delayed Price Discovery and Stale Trends: Efficient markets require continuous, incremental data to price assets accurately. Allowing companies to report semi-annually means equity prices will trend for months on entirely obsolete narratives. This creates an unlevel playing field where asymmetric information risk thrives, directly disadvantaging retail investors who do not have access to alternative data pipelines.

The Critical Function of Frequent Cadences: Regular, predictable, quarterly touchpoints ensure that public companies behave with consistent transparency. While a three-month cadence requires discipline, it ensures that management remains accountable to its baseline operating targets in real-time. In contrast, a six-month cycle represents an eternity in modern, algorithmic markets, completely breaking the historical baseline of corporate accountability.

While proponents argue that quarterly reporting places an administrative burden on companies, the infrastructure for modern corporate accounting is highly automated. The compliance cost of compiling quarterly data is a minor, necessary premium paid to maintain public investor trust and market liquidity.

Frequent, structured transparency is fundamental to a well-functioning market. I strongly urge the Commission to reject the proposed Form 10-S and maintain the current mandatory quarterly reporting framework to protect investors and maintain orderly, transparent markets.

Sincerely,

Ryan Schaefer
Individual Investor

57:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Erik Swanson

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commission's proposal to permit certain issuers to report on a semiannual basis rather than quarterly. Reducing the frequency of required disclosures would materially weaken the transparency framework that underpins U.S. capital markets.

Quarterly reporting has long served as a foundational mechanism for ensuring that investors receive timely, decision-useful information about an issuer's financial condition, risk exposures, and operational performance. Moving to a semiannual cadence would create extended periods in which material developments may go unreported, increasing information asymmetry between insiders and the investing public. This would undermine market efficiency and elevate the risk of mispricing, volatility, and unequal access to critical data.

The Commission has repeatedly emphasized that transparency is essential to investor protection and to maintaining confidence in U.S. markets. Allowing issuers to report only twice per year runs counter to that principle. In an environment where business conditions, macroeconomic factors, and risk profiles can shift rapidly, less frequent reporting would leave investors with outdated information for longer periods and impair their ability to make informed decisions.

While the proposal suggests that reduced reporting may lessen compliance burdens, any marginal administrative benefit is outweighed by the significant costs to market integrity. The existing quarterly framework strikes an appropriate balance between issuer obligations and investor needs, and it should be preserved.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed rule permitting semiannual reporting. Maintaining robust, frequent disclosure requirements is essential to protecting investors and sustaining the transparency that has long distinguished U.S. capital markets.

Thank you for considering this comment.

58:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Henggao Cai
From: Henggao Cai

I am submitting this comment as an individual investor and member of the public. I write in strong opposition to this proposal. While framed as a modernization of an outdated framework, this rule would, in practice, reduce the flow of standardized financial information that retail investors depend on to make decisions and it would do so for the primary benefit of corporate management teams, not the investors the Commission is charged with protecting.

The SEC's core mandate under the Securities Exchange Act is to protect investors, maintain fair and orderly markets, and facilitate capital formation. This proposal inverts that priority. It privileges corporate convenience over investor access, and it does so without credible evidence that quarterly reporting is actually causing the harms its critics claim.

The short-term argument does not hold up. The central justification for this rule is that quarterly reporting forces managers into short-term decision-making. This argument has been repeated for years by executives and lobbyists, but the empirical record does not support it. Academic research examining companies that shifted to semiannual reporting in the UK and EU found no meaningful improvement in long-term investment behavior. What it did find was increased stock price volatility, investor overreaction in the absence of own-firm disclosures, and overcorrection when semiannual reports finally arrived. The Commission is proposing to restructure a 50-year disclosure regime based on a premise that peer-reviewed research has already substantially undermined.

Retail investors will bear the cost of this rule. Institutional investors, hedge funds, and company insiders do not depend on quarterly 10-Q filings the way retail investors do. They have access to earnings calls, analyst relationships, roadshows, and proprietary data services. Retail investors, the people this agency exists to protect, rely on standardized, mandatory disclosures as their primary window into how a company is actually performing. Doubling the gap between those disclosures from three months to six creates a structural information advantage for sophisticated participants and leaves ordinary investors operating on assumptions for longer periods. This is not a theoretical concern. It is a predictable and documented outcome.

The Form 8-K backstop is not an adequate substitute. The proposal appears to rely on current-event 8-K filings to bridge the information gap between semiannual reports. But 8-K filings are management-discretionary, non-standardized, and cannot substitute for the systematic financial data, such as revenue, earnings, cash flow, balance sheet changes that Form 10-Q requires. A company that elects semiannual reporting could go six months without giving investors a standardized, auditable view of its financial condition, entirely within the rules as proposed.

The "optional" framing obscures the aggregate harm. Because this is presented as a voluntary election, proponents argue that market forces will discipline companies that reduce disclosure. That argument fails for several reasons. First, retail investors often cannot easily identify which companies have switched to semiannual reporting or understand the implications when they do. Second, the companies most likely to elect semiannual reporting are smaller, less-covered firms where information is already scarce, which is precisely where retail investors are most exposed. Third, once semiannual reporting becomes normalized, investor expectations will adjust downward and the transparency premium will erode.

I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal. If the Commission is genuinely concerned about compliance costs for smaller issuers, there are targeted ways to address that and streamlining the content requirements of Form 10-Q, extending filing deadlines for non-accelerated filers, or providing technical assistance without eliminating the quarterly disclosure cadence that has underpinned market confidence for over half a century.

Flexibility is not a regulatory virtue when the costs fall on those with the least access to alternative information. I ask the Commission to reject this rule and reaffirm its commitment to investor protection as its primary obligation.

Thank you for considering this comment.
Henggao Cai

63:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: John Flynn Jr.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal that would allow U.S. public companies to report earnings on a semiannual basis instead of quarterly.

Quarterly reporting is one of the few mechanisms that ensures timely transparency, market discipline, and early detection of financial stress. Reducing reporting frequency would:

Increase information asymmetry between insiders and the investing public
Delay the visibility of deteriorating fundamentals, allowing problems to compound before investors can react
Reduce accountability for management teams who already face limited scrutiny between earnings cycles
Increase volatility, as markets would be forced to digest twice as much information at once
Weaken U.S. market leadership, which has historically been built on rigorous disclosure standards

The argument that quarterly reporting creates "short-termism" is overstated. Companies already have tools to guide long-term expectations without reducing transparency. Investors, analysts, and regulators rely on quarterly data to monitor risk, capital allocation, and operational performance. Semiannual reporting would remove a critical safeguard.

For these reasons, I urge the Commission to withdraw or substantially reconsider this proposal. The costs to market integrity and investor protection far outweigh any claimed benefits.

Thank you for considering this comment.
John Flynn Jr.

72:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Peter Ford

1. Information gaps create pricing inefficiencies and risk. Quarterly earnings have been the bedrock of fundamental analysis for decades. Cutting reporting to twice a year means investors go six months without an auditor-reviewed financial picture of a company. For individual investors, that's blind flying. For advisors managing client portfolios, it means extended periods where you simply don't know if the thesis on a position has changed. Professional advisors who rely on 10-Q data for position monitoring, valuation updates, and risk flagging would face a material degradation in signal quality.

2. "Optional" won't stay optional for long and the wrong companies will opt in. The framing as optional makes this sound benign. But in practice, companies with something to hide between periods. Those with deteriorating margins, rising debt, operational problems are the most motivated to elect semiannual filing. High-quality, stable businesses have little to gain. This creates adverse selection: the universe of semiannual filers will be skewed toward companies with elevated opacity risk, right at the moment investors have less visibility.

3. It undermines earnings-based risk management. Professional advisors use quarterly filings for far more than earnings: they track working capital trends, debt covenant headroom, revenue seasonality, segment-level performance, and management guidance credibility. A six-month gap makes all of this harder. Sector-sensitive portfolios where conditions shift materially quarter to quarter would be particularly exposed.

4. The market efficiency argument cuts the other way. Proponents (echoing Chairman Atkins' language about companies determining "the interim reporting frequency that best serves their business needs") frame this as reducing burden. But capital markets are efficient because information flows continuously and uniformly. Allowing a two-tier system fragments that. Stocks of semiannual filers would likely trade at a discount — a permanent information-risk premium, which ironically hurts the very companies that elect the option.

79:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Anonymous

I respectfully oppose the proposal to permit public companies to replace quarterly reporting with semiannual reporting.

Quarterly reporting plays a critical role in maintaining transparency, accountability, and market confidence. It provides investors with a regular, structured view of operating performance, liquidity, and emerging risks. Extending the reporting cadence to six months increases the likelihood that important trends or deteriorations remain formally undisclosed for longer periods, potentially amplifying their impact when information eventually surfaces.

The proposal's reliance on Form 8-K disclosures as a mitigating measure is insufficient. Event-driven filings are reactive by design and do not provide the comprehensive, comparable, and trend-based insights that periodic reports offer. Quarterly reports allow investors to assess directionality and context, not just isolated events.

Optional semiannual reporting also raises concerns around comparability and information symmetry. A mixed environment — where some issuers report quarterly and others semiannually — complicates peer analysis and may disadvantage less-resourced investors who rely on standardized disclosures. Over time, reduced reporting frequency may also weaken internal reporting discipline and delay identification and remediation of accounting or control issues.

If the Commission's objective is to modernize and reduce unnecessary burden, a more balanced approach would be to streamline quarterly reporting requirements rather than reduce reporting frequency altogether. Preserving the quarterly cadence remains important to ensuring orderly markets, effective price discovery, and sustained investor trust.
80:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Bayo Olabisi

I think this idea of 10-S reporting is not a good policy in terms of trust in corporate financial disclosures. The US stock market is a shining example for the world, it runs on up to date information about listed companies and accurate, timely reports of business activities, forecasts are often updated during these quarterly reports.

The quarterly reporting system has worked for decades and isn't broken. And the pace of new financial information in the era of AI is only getting faster, all while the burden of reporting isn't more onerous. We have seen the markets react with volatile surprise to delayed economic information release by days or weeks on the part of government, one can only imagine the double surprise from an extra quarter of delay by listed companies.

Change is good, but not every change is including this one. To change a working system in order to allow a few potentially unscrupulous entities to take advantage will make it difficult for the investing public to understand essential status of their business activities, and to make an apples to apples assessment of how their businesses are doing.

Even if SEC is persuaded a small group of investment fund companies and perhaps others will anticipate this semiannual report model works for them, it could do a trial run of it for this group to see how stock markets react to it. But making the option available to the entire market without a careful experimental dry run carries more risks than rewards - less transparency, less inter/intra sector comparability, more potential for fraud, and malfeasance. These are concerns for the regulatory oversight and a trusted reporting system.
81:
Subject: File Number S7-2026-15
From: Marc Steinberg

May 9, 2026

As a former SEC attorney who served in the Division of Enforcement and the Office of General Counsel, I write in my personal capacity to oppose the Commission's proposed amendments to allow companies to file semiannual, rather than quarterly, reports. Our securities markets are based on disclosure of material information on a timely basis, thereby seeking to enhance market integrity and investor protection. This proposal materially impedes these objectives.

It seems as if the Commission will adopt these proposals in its deregulatory mode of focus. If it elects to do so, the very least that should be effectuated is the amendment of the Form 8-K to include additional important developments that must be timely disclosed. For example, current Form 8-K does not require the prompt disclosure of the loss of a material contract. With semi-annual reporting, this important fact will not reach investors until at least six months after the loss of such contract. This is clearly unacceptable.

I draw your attention please to my award-winning Oxford University Press book Rethinking Securities Law (2021) (awarded Winner best law book of 2021 by the American Book Fest Awards). In undertaking this mode of deregulation, it is incumbent upon the SEC to act as a responsible regulator. In that regard, the minimum that the Commission should undertake is to amend the Form 8-K to help ensure that material information is not embargoed for an unduly long period of time, redounding to the detriment of the U.S. securities markets, financial intermediaries, and investors.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,
Marc I. Steinberg
Radford Chair in Law and Professor of Law
SMU Dedman School of Law
82:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Anonymous

Quarterly reporting data is critical for all investors ranging from institutional investors to retail investors. This data allows them to make well-informed decisions about capital allocation. Maintaining quarterly reporting requirements is crucial for transparency and trust in the US financial markets. I firmly believe that altering the current quarterly disclosure rules would be detrimental to all investors.

That said, if changes are deemed necessary, it is essential that companies continue to provide key financial statements on a quarterly basis. These include the cash flow statement, balance sheet, and income statement. Additionally, investors must receive up-to-date information on the number of shares outstanding, segment revenues, and detailed disclosures regarding any debt or borrowing. While some elements of the current quarterly filings, such as aspects of the 10-Q, may seem redundant, the core financial data mentioned above remain vital for investors' ability to assess a company's health and prospects.

The strength and integrity of the United States economy and its stock market have been built on the reliability of this information investors receive.

The argument that reducing quarterly reporting would benefit smaller companies is wrong. It's precisely these companies that need to report more frequently, as smaller companies undergo more rapid structural changes than large corporations which is why they need to keep investors adequately informed.

In conclusion, I strongly advocate for preserving the existing quarterly reporting obligations. However, if modifications are made, ensuring the continued quarterly disclosure of the financial data listed above is critical to maintaining investor confidence in the United States.

87:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Michael Mosquera

I have been a private investor for more than half my life. The American stock exchanges are the most liquid, efficient and with some exceptions, the most transparent and secure. However I do see two serious issues jeopardizing the competitive strengths of American public corporations.

First is short term thinking. In many cases, management is focused on exceeding quarterly expectations at the expense of long term growth. However management that focused on long term growth have built some of America's iconic corporations. Amazon, Netflix and Eli Lilly come to mind.

Second, a lack of proper oversight of management by the board of directors. In many cases, boards of directors have become rubber stamps for management. This lack of oversight eventually leads to significant corporate failures. Enron, Tyco and WorldCom come to mind.

I propose two corporate reporting options that the shareholders vote on every three years.

The first option is status quo. Change nothing with the current corporate reporting requirements.

The second option is to allow corporate reporting on an annual basis. However significant enhancements to oversight of management must be implemented.

1. Senior management such as the CEO, CFO and COO are barred from serving on the board of directors. Cannot allow the fox to watch the hen house.

2. The board of directors must be held to a higher standard of fiduciary responsibility and can be personally be held liable if they are negligent in this responsibility.

3. Significantly reduce the amount of required public reporting.

4. A section in the annual report must be required to highlight the fiduciary actions taken by the board of directors.

Giving public corporations greater flexibility in reporting requirements while requiring enhanced oversight by corporate boards will encourage management to concentrate on long term growth.

93:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Kenneth I Kornbluh

May 11, 2026

To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I submit this comment in opposition to the proposed amendments under File Number S7-2026-15, which would permit public companies to file semiannual reports in lieu of quarterly reports.

I manage a hedge fund operating a series of five funds with hundreds of millions of dollars in assets belonging to individual investors. Our investment process depends heavily on quarterly reports, annual reports, and all public company disclosures. This proposed rule change would directly and materially harm our ability to make informed investment decisions on behalf of our clients.

Quarterly earnings figures and forward-looking guidance are central to how we evaluate companies and construct our portfolios. We rely on these numbers not only to assess a company's current financial health, but to compare actual results against prior estimates and to track management's consistency and accuracy in projecting future performance. Reducing reporting to a semiannual cadence would double the information gap between reporting periods, leaving investors to make consequential decisions with materially less data — and giving company insiders a significant and troubling informational advantage over the markets.

This informational asymmetry is already a serious concern under the current quarterly regime. Company insiders — executives, board members, and others with access to internal data — know their company's financial trajectory months before the public does. They observe revenue trends, cost pressures, and operational developments in real time. Public investors, by contrast, must wait for scheduled disclosures. Moving to semiannual reporting would dramatically widen this gap, extending the period during which insiders hold material non-public information to an even greater degree.

Rather than moving in this direction, the Commission should consider whether an additional lighter and more frequent reporting regime — such as mandatory monthly disclosure of key performance metrics — might serve investors and market integrity. Some of the world's best-managed companies already voluntarily provide this level of transparency, demonstrating that it is both achievable and valuable.

Equally important to us is the qualitative dimension of quarterly reporting: the earnings calls and commentary delivered by senior executives in their own voices. These are irreplaceable. The way a CEO or CFO speaks about their business — their tone, their confidence, their command of the details — provides insight that no financial statement alone can convey. It allows sophisticated investors to assess management quality, detect uncertainty or overconfidence, and make more nuanced judgments about long-term prospects. Cutting the frequency of these interactions in half would be a serious loss to the investment community.

The United States' dominance in global financial markets is not accidental. It is built on a foundation of robust, frequent, and reliable public disclosure requirements. Quarterly reporting is a cornerstone of that foundation. It gives investors confidence that the companies they invest in are being held to consistent standards of transparency. Reducing that frequency undermines market fairness, erodes investor trust, and weakens the competitive advantage that U.S. markets have long held globally.

I strongly urge the Commission to reject this proposed rule change and instead consider moving in the opposite direction — toward more frequent and more transparent disclosure — in order to better protect investors and preserve the integrity of U.S. capital markets.

Respectfully submitted,
Ken Kornbluh
CEO
Kambio Partners LLC
94:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Nathan Powers

I am writing this on behalf of a large and growing chorus of regular everyday investors who would be harmed by a recently proposed rule change to the quarterly reporting system. My reasoning for being strongly against this change is as follows.

The long and storied tradition of quarterly reporting is the foundation that our current financial system relies on to keep publicly traded companies honest. To do away with this system would lead to profoundly bad outcomes as certain bad actors would be allowed to hide their misdeeds for longer and this change will result in extra unnecessary volatility in public markets.

And it's not just the large multi year frauds like Enron, Worldcom, Nikola, Wirecard, and others, it is the small almost invisible statements that are the bigger problem. Companies such as Alphabet, Under Armour, and Wells Fargo have all recently settled complaints from shareholders in regards to misleading investors in their companies. If we go to a system where companies are only required to show their books only twice per year it is easily conceivable that this minor form of fraud would be grow in scale and go undetected for longer.

This change would go deeper than just allowing hucksters and stock salesman to benefit it would also be disastrous for retail investors and perhaps even institutions. Many companies already experience tremendous volatility around their quarterly earnings reports, making the requirement only twice per year would be detonating a nuclear bomb. the share price of companies would likely collapse or soar on these infrequent reports.

I ask that you keep the status quo as it is. Quarterly reports are foundational in making sure that those who seek to misuse and abuse the system are found out quickly. It would also be beneficial to all investors to not be forced to stomach large swings in the price of their owned securities.

Thank You for your attention to this matter.

In an era of open information, having less data that allows investors to make informed decisions - whether short or long term - is a step backwards that permits public companies a brief moment of regulatory respite they agreed was worth dealing with when going public in the first place.

Switching to semi-annual reporting dilutes this trust and harms the general public who rely on this information.
99:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Andrew Parker

Re: File No. S7-2026-15 — Proposed Amendments Permitting Optional Semiannual Reporting

To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule permitting optional semiannual reporting for eligible public companies.

This comment responds specifically to:

Request for Comment 18: "Would this practice create any new or heightened investor protection concerns?"

and

Request for Comment 26: "For semiannual filers, what impact would a shift to semiannual reporting have on: (1) companies' disclosure controls and procedures, (2) companies' internal control over financial reporting, and (3) independent public accountants' strategy and approach for the annual audit of companies' internal control over financial reporting or financial statements? With semiannual reporting, is there a potential for a material increase in the risk that material misstatements (either due to error or fraud) or control deficiencies are not timely detected or communicated to the independent public accountant thereby limiting potential remediation of these issues by the issuer? Please provide any data related to these questions."

There are conditions that based on my experience when present generate serious concern for a material increase in the risk that material misstatements (either due to error or fraud) or control deficiencies are not timely detected by or communicated to the independent public accountant thereby limiting potential remediation of these issues by the issuer. These conditions also indicate new or heightened investor protection concerns when coupled with semiannual reporting.

A shift to semi-annual reporting under the current disclosure requirements for public companies with recent material revisions, material misstatements, and material period-end financial reporting control failures especially when coupled with material significant deficiencies in ICFR at year-ends presents serious risks when:

1) The compounding effect of material extraordinary risks to financial reporting from fraud or operational problems that present themselves as material control failures to period end financial reporting, occurring often alongside near-material failures in ICFRs such as operational controls for material line items at the entity level that are sometimes even remediated in the last quarter or classified as significant deficiencies at year-end and avoid public disclosure at all. The effect of current disclosure practices results in companies avoiding public reporting of significant deficiencies, including some longstanding issues, that often occur alongside recent period-end financial reporting control failures under current disclosure law through classification as a significant deficiency at year end (not explicitly required to be disclosed at all sometimes, but disclosure might occur under current materiality principles). Significant deficiencies can be widespread in both ICFR operational and period end financial reporting controls after an ICFR control failure is removed from public disclosure. While the condition alone of an ICFR material failure in period end financial reporting in my view should prevent semiannual reporting, a significant deficiency in an operational ICFR can linger after the period-end financial reporting control has been removed from public view in disclosures in the 10-K. This information is decision useful because conditions listed above may lead to unexpected costs to investors in material line items like those that could be directly used to value the company in the event of going concern risk that may appear in the future. Late filing slightly complicates the picture and rules should also be considered to reduce the burden for these companies in the short rather than medium-term which semi-annual reporting might accomplish.

2) There are longer lead times until corrective action will be taken in practice towards most now undisclosed revisions due to the current frequency of management quarterly reviews and inquiry from auditors as compared to semiannual reporting. Semiannual reporting would give companies much less chances over time to put into practice corrective action which sometimes may take a couple years to get correct. Quarterly reporting entails more frequent account reconciliations that often help discover errors in practice, management reviews, and important conversations quarterly about material balances that prevent surprises with unnecessary costs/overruns at year end that are common in emerging growth companies with these conditions present.

These risks are hard for retail investors to assess fully under the current disclosure requirements, especially the disclosures made in practice. Coupled with semiannual reporting, the current disclosure requirements seem very inadequate to protect all investors' interests.

I think that narrowing the scope of who will be eligible for semiannual reporting to not include public companies with these conditions present for at least three years after they are present will increase capital markets efficiency and decrease the administrative burden on companies with proper corporate reporting and governance priorities. A negative effect to information provided would be from decreased comparability and consistency in information provided by companies and in between years. A forced change would probably be problematic for these reasons but adjustments could possibly be provided by the companies if possible.

Important Considerations for Semi-Annual Reporting

1. Revisions when in total material over a three year period because of the compounding effect through quarters of most minor errors seen in practice. Materiality should be considered over at least a three year rolling period to prevent intentional one year shifts and one-off operational errors leading to an increased administrative burden that is unproductive to protect investor interests. Disclosures should be made considering qualitative factors like those listed in SEC Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 99 (SAB 99). Disclosures about the scope of total revisions to retail investors are often not provided in practice since some may be disclosed exclusively to institutional investors. Total quantitatively would be decision-useful information provided to retail investors about the company's progress toward improving corporate governance.

2. Material Restatements: Important to mention with consideration of qualitative factors like those listed in SEC Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 99 (SAB 99).

3. Period End Financial reporting material control failures in the past three years and otherwise undisclosed ICFR operational control failure remediation affecting material line items when occurring in the last quarter or still classified as a significant deficiency at year end for those following three years. These disclosures would also need to address steps taken to improve information technology when appropriate. Material control remediation in the last quarter of the year that is not subject to proper risk assessment, when combined with recent failures in period end financial reporting, present fraud and operational risks that should be disclosed to shareholders. Both problems indicate semiannual reporting will not adequately protect investor interests due to compounding effects of material fraud and operational risk.

Excluding companies that exhibit risky characteristics from semiannual reporting is key to address concerns that semiannual reporting will make fraud or operational problems harder to detect quickly based on practical experience with following control failures and resulting impacts to retail investors. Narrowing the rules will also provide additional incentives for companies to avoid improperly taking advantage of these conditions that are common in emerging growth companies and promote proper governance practices.

Impact on Public Companies

What percentage of emerging growth companies have control failures in ICFR? As of 2017, among the 1,862 EGC filers, 1,355 provided a management report on internal control over financial reporting in their most recent annual filing. Of those 1,355 companies, approximately 46% reported material weaknesses.

Adverse ICFR Disclosure Rate by company size (FY2022 excerpt):
- Large Accelerated Filers: 5.8%
- Accelerated Filers: 17.7%
- Non-Accelerated Filers: 38.0%

Regards,
Andrew Parker
